questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the physician by his parents because he continues to wet the bed approximately five times weekly. He has never had a sustained dry period longer than 7 days. Most of the "accidents" occur during the first 3 hours of the night and occasionally during dreams in which he imagines himself urinating. He is afraid to go to friends' houses for sleepovers because of this problem. He worries that his friends would make fun of him if they knew, and he frequently feels bad about hims,elf "because I pee the bed like a baby." He was diagnosed with type 1 diabetes mellitus 1 year ago. It is well controlled with insulin, and he is compliant with the medication regimen. Examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's bed-wetting?

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Urinary overflow incontinence secondary to diabetes mellitus

questions.educationalObjective: Primary enuresis refers to episodic urinary incontinence occurring in children older than 5 years. The episodes commonly lead to adjustment problems and low self-esteem. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Primary enuresis refers to episodic urinary incontinence occurring in children older than 5 years. The episodes commonly lead to adjustment problems and low self-esteem. The pathogenesis of primary enuresis most commonly involves a delay in maturation, though it may alternatively be related to a small functional bladder capacity or genetic factors. Along with a voiding diary, patients with nocturnal enuresis should undergo a thorough history, physical examination, and urinalysis to rule out medical causes (eg, bladder dysfunction, urinary tract infection). Many patients with primary enuresis spontaneously improve, though motivational therapy (eg, rewarding the patient for urinating prior to bed or having incontinence-free nights) may hasten improvement. In patients with persistent enuresis or enuresis associated with emotional problems, management with enuresis alarms or desmopressin is indicated.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Generalized anxiety disorder (Choice A) is an anxiety disorder that features excessive worrying about a range of topics with associated functional impairment. This patient's worry is specific to nocturnal urinary incontinence. Oppositional defiant disorder (Choice B) manifests as a persistent pattern of angry mood, defiant behavior, and vindictiveness that causes distress and impaired functioning in the child or their caretakers. This child's enuresis is not a voluntary behavior. Social phobia (Choice D), also known as social anxiety disorder, refers to an excessive fear of scrutiny, embarrassment, or rejection in social or performance situations leading to significant distress or impaired functioning. This patient's anxiety in social situations is secondary to enuresis. Lab Values Calculator A) Generalized anxiety disorder B) Oppositional defiant disorder C) Primary enuresis D) Social phobia E) Urinary overflow incontinence secondary to diabetes mellitus Correct Answer: C. questions.stem: A 77-year-old woman is brought to the physician by her daughter because of a 3-year history of worsening forgetfulness. The patient says that she has not had any problems and does not know why her daughter is so concerned. She is able to recall zero of three objects after 5 minutes. She has significant deficits in praxis, verbal category fluency, and naming. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies are within the reference range. Which of the following neurotransmitters is most closely associated with the pathophysiology of this patient's illness?
questions.educationalObjective: Dementia, Alzheimer type, results from decreased cholinergic signaling in the cerebral cortex and other brain areas. It is the most prevalent dementia, presenting with progressive cognitive decline that culminates in the inability to perform basic activities of daily living. Cholinesterase inhibitors such as donepezil are the first-line treatment. Lab Values Calculator

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Dementia, Alzheimer type, results from decreased cholinergic signaling. Cholinergic neurons, which primarily originate from the locus coeruleus and basal forebrain, project widely to areas including the cerebral cortex to mediate attention, learning, and memory. In dementia, Alzheimer type, a proteinopathy involving amyloid and tau proteins leads to the degeneration of cholinergic neurons. Dementia, Alzheimer type, is the most prevalent dementia, presenting with progressive cognitive decline that begins with short-term memory impairment and decline in visuospatial abilities, and progresses to apraxia, language abnormalities, and dysexecutive syndrome. The disease culminates in behavioral and personality changes that prevent the patient from performing basic activities of daily living such as bathing. Acetylcholinesterase normally hydrolyzes acetylcholine in synaptic clefts. Donepezil and other cholinesterase inhibitors noncompetitively and reversibly inhibit acetylcholinesterase activity and thereby increase the amount of synaptic acetylcholine available for neurotransmission. Though donepezil may slow the rate of cognitive decline and modestly improve functionality, donepezil and the other cholinesterase inhibitors are not curative.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: y-Aminobutyric acid (Choice B), GABA, is the primary inhibitory neurotransmitter. GABA deficiency is implicated in diseases of central nervous system hyperexcitability but not dementia, Alzheimer type. Dopamine (Choice C) is a monoamine neurotransmitter that mediates reward in the mesolimbic pathway and movement in the basal ganglia. Dopamine excess in the mesolimbic pathway is implicated in psychosis. Dopamine deficiency in the basal ganglia leads to parkinsonism. Dopamine is not known to play a significant role in the pathogenesis of dementia, Alzheimer type. Lab Values Calculator Correct Answer: A. The disease culminates in behavioral and personality changes that prevent the patient from performing basic activities of daily living such as bathing. GABA deficiency is implicated in diseases of central nervous system hyperexcitability but not dementia, Alzheimer type. Dopamine (Choice C) is a monoamine neurotransmitter that mediates reward in the mesolimbic pathway and movement in the basal ganglia. Dopamine excess in the mesolimbic pathway is implicated in psychosis. Dopamine deficiency in the basal ganglia leads to parkinsonism. Glutamate excess leads to neuronal excitotoxicity and has been implicated in neurodegenerative diseases such as dementia, Alzheimer type, though glutamate's precise role in this type of dementia is unclear. Norepinephrine (Choice E) is a catecholamine hormone and neurotransmitter. Its primary role in the brain is to promote alertness. Attention-deficit/hyperactivity syndrome involves norepinephrine dysregulation, but dementia, Alzheimer type, is not believed to involve norepinephrine signaling. Serotonin (Choice F) is a monoamine neurotransmitter involved in mood regulation and implicated in mood disorders. Serotonin does not play a known large role in dementia, Alzheimer type.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with Parkinson disease is brought to the physician by his wife for a follow-up examination 6 weeks after pramipexole was added to his regimen of carbidopa-levodopa to manage increasingly severe bradykinesia. The patient reports improved movement and greater stability with walking. His wife says that he has become increasingly suspicious of her and has accused her of infidelity. Last week, she awoke during the night and found him sitting in the living room with his hunting rifle on his knees. The patient had insisted that a prowler or "her paramour" was sneaking around the house, and he intended to "teach him a lesson he won't have to learn twice." The patient has no history of psychiatric illness, but his wife says he has seemed depressed and has had decreased appetite and motivation during the past 4 weeks. The patient just shrugs his shoulders in response to his wife's comment. He has hypertension controlled with lisinopril and hyperlipidemia controlled with lovastatin. His blood pressure is 138/84 mm Hg. Examination shows less rigidity and increased arm swing compared with examination results 6 weeks ago. On mental status examination, he is cooperative and superficially polite. He has a mildly dysphoric mood and restricted affect. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's change in mental status?
questions.educationalObjective: Pramipexole nonselectively increases dopamine concentrations in the basal ganglia and limbic system, leading to improvement of Parkinson symptoms along with the risk of psychotic symptoms, respectively. Mood dysregulation may also be demonstrated. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Pramipexole increases dopamine concentrations in several brain areas. In the dorsal striatum, an increased dopamine concentration leads to improvement in Parkinson symptoms. In the ventral striatum, dopamine-enhancing medications may lead to mood dysregulation or depression (commonly manifesting with somatic or neurovegetative symptoms). In the limbic system, an increased dopamine concentration leads to psychotic symptoms such as paranoid delusions, hallucinations, and an incongruent affect (as demonstrated by this patient). Distressing psychotic symptoms in the setting of Parkinson disease treatment are typically managed with antipsychotic medication or a decreased dose of the inciting medication.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Delusional disorder (Choice B) is characterized by the presence of one or more delusions (fixed, false beliefs) for a month or longer without other psychotic symptoms. This patient does not demonstrate one circumscribed delusion, instead presenting with general paranoia (against his wife, her "paramour," a prowler). Further, the onset of psychotic symptoms correlates with the initiation of pramipexole. Major depressive disorder (Choice C) includes 2 or more weeks of five of the following symptoms: depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, psychomotor retardation, suicidal thoughts, and/or neurovegetative symptoms (decreased Lab Values Calculator D) Paranoid personality disorder E) Progression of Parkinson disease Correct Answer: A. Pramipexole increases dopamine concentrations in several brain areas. In the dorsal striatum, an increased dopamine concentration leads to improvement in Parkinson symptoms. In the ventral striatum, dopamine-enhancing medications may lead to mood dysregulation or depression (commonly manifesting with somatic or neurovegetative symptoms). In the limbic system, an increased dopamine concentration leads to psychotic symptoms such as paranoid delusions, hallucinations, and an incongruent affect (as demonstrated by this patient). Distressing psychotic symptoms in the setting of Parkinson disease treatment are typically managed with antipsychotic medication or a decreased dose of the inciting medication. Incorrect Answers: B, C, D, and E. Delusional disorder (Choice B) is characterized by the presence of one or more delusions (fixed, false beliefs) for a month or longer without other psychotic symptoms. This patient does not demonstrate one circumscribed delusion, instead presenting with general paranoia (against his wife, her "paramour," a prowler). Further, the onset of psychotic symptoms correlates with the initiation of pramipexole. Major depressive disorder (Choice C) includes 2 or more weeks of five of the following symptoms: depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, psychomotor retardation, suicidal thoughts, and/or neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance). By definition, the symptoms are not related to an underlying medical condition or a medication. The onset of this patient's mood changes correlates with the initiation of pramipexole. Paranoid personality disorder (Choice D) is a cluster A personality disorder, the odd or eccentric cluster, characterized by a pervasive mistrust of others and viewing others as possessing malicious intent. Personality disorders by definition are persistent. This patient's paranoia does not precede the initiation of pramipexole. The progression of Parkinson disease (Choice E) commonly leads to dementia. However, neuropsychiatric symptoms such as depression and anxiety are common both early and late in the disease. As well, the onset of this patient's psychotic symptoms correlates with the initiation of pramipexole.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her mother, who reports that the patient has been threatening her at home during the past 2 weeks. The patient has a 5-year history of schizophrenia. Her psychiatric symptoms had been "fairly" well controlled with oral quetiapine for 6 months, but she reports that she stopped taking her medication 6 weeks ago because it was sedating her. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Mental status examination shows an agitated mood and a guarded affect. She appears to be responding to internal stimuli. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in pharmacotherapy?
questions.educationalObjective: In patients experiencing an incomplete response and unacceptable side effects (eg, side effects leading to nonadherence) to a psychiatric medication, a different medication should be favored over a dose increase. In patients experiencing sedation on quetiapine, a less sedating antipsychotic such as aripiprazole should be considered. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Schizophrenia is characterized by delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, and negative symptoms (eg, alogia, apathy, flattened affect). Treatment requires long-term antipsychotic medication. In patients with a partial response to an antipsychotic, a dose increase should be considered. However, intolerable side effects may limit the maximum acceptable dose. Quetiapine is an atypical antipsychotic medication that primarily antagonizes dopamine-2 receptors and serotonin receptors. Quetiapine also antagonizes histamine receptors, adrenoreceptors, and muscarinic receptors, leading to sedation, weight gain, orthostatic hypotension, and anticholinergic side effects (eg, dry mouth, urinary retention, constipation). This patient experiencing the combination of intolerable sedation (leading to nonadherence) and incomplete control of psychotic symptoms should be trialed on a less sedating antipsychotic medication. Aripiprazole is an antipsychotic medication with a lower affinity for histamine and acetylcholine receptors and is therefore less likely to cause sedation.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Increasing the dosage of quetiapine (Choice A) would likely worsen this patient's sedation. She has already self-discontinued quetiapine because of sedation, so increasing the dosage would make her even less likely to adhere to the medication regimen. Switching from quetiapine to amitriptyline (Choice B) or bupropion (Choice D), antidepressant medications, would not address this patient's psychotic symptoms. This patient should be switched to a different antipsychotic medication. Switching from quetiapine to chlorpromazine (Choice E) would be less likely to resolve the patient's sedation. Chlorpromazine is a low- potency typical antipsychotic that antagonizes histamine receptors, adrenoreceptors, and muscarinic receptors. It commonly causes sedation. questions.stem:
An 86-year-old woman is brought to the physician because of confusion, forgetfulness, and lethargy for 6 weeks. She lives by herself and has been functioning independently. She has hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, and osteoarthritis of the lumbar spine and hips. Her drug regimen includes hydrochlorothiazide, captopril, naproxen, gemfibrozil, and lovastatin. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 87/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Reflexes bilaterally are brisk, and muscle strength in the upper and lower extremities is excellent. On mental status examination, she is oriented to person but not to time and place and is unable to remember three objects after 2 minutes; she cannot perform serial sevens and is frustrated when she cannot answer proverb or judgment questions. Laboratory studies show:
[FIGURE: q05_lab1]
Toxicology screening is negative. A CT scan of the head shows mild diffuse cerebral atrophy with no lesions. An x-ray of the chest shows normal findings. Which of the following is the most likely cause of the change in mental status?

figureRefs.description: Laboratory values table with hematocrit, leukocyte count, and serum chemistry values.

questions.educationalObjective: Thiazide diuretics, such as hydrochlorothiazide, can cause clinically significant hyponatremia. Chronic hyponatremia can present with lethargy, fatigue, nausea, forgetfulness, and confusion. Treatment includes discontinuing the causative medication and restricting free water intake until sodium concentrations return to normal. Lab Values Calculator

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Electrolyte derangements such as hyponatremia or hypernatremia can cause altered mental status. Hyponatremia can be acute or chronic. Acute hyponatremia occurs in 24 to 48 hours and commonly presents with symptoms of nausea, malaise, lethargy, obtundation or coma, and seizures secondary to cerebral edema, as water is drawn into the neuronal cells in the setting of hypotonic interstitial fluid. Given the rapid decline in sodium, symptoms can even present with sodium concentrations of 130 mEq/L. In chronic hyponatremia, however, symptoms generally do not present until the hyponatremia has become significant (usually less than 120 mEq/L). Symptoms include fatigue, nausea, forgetfulness, lethargy, and confusion. Hydrochlorothiazide and other thiazide diuretics can induce •••• -■- Lab Values Calculator Correct Answer: B. Electrolyte derangements such as hyponatremia or hypernatremia can cause altered mental status. Hyponatremia can be acute or chronic. Acute hyponatremia occurs in 24 to 48 hours and commonly presents with symptoms of nausea, malaise, lethargy, obtundation or coma, and seizures secondary to cerebral edema, as water is drawn into the neuronal cells in the setting of hypotonic interstitial fluid. Given the rapid decline in sodium, symptoms can even present with sodium concentrations of 130 mEq/L. In chronic hyponatremia, however, symptoms generally do not present until the hyponatremia has become significant (usually less than 120 mEq/L). Symptoms include fatigue, nausea, forgetfulness, lethargy, and confusion. Hydrochlorothiazide and other thiazide diuretics can induce hyponatremia because of impaired urine dilution. Since they act on sodium-chloride symporters in the distal tubule, they do not affect the medullary gradient or the action of antidiuretic hormone. Therefore, these patients can lose sodium in excess of water leading to euvolemic (but also hypovolemic) hyponatremia. The loss of excess solutes in relation to the loss of water also explains this patient's hypokalemia and hypochloremia. Treatment of diuretic-induced hyponatremia involves discontinuing the causative agent, restoring euvolemia if the patient is not euvolemic, and restricting free water intake.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Adverse effect of captopril therapy (Choice A) is incorrect. Angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, such as captopril, are used in the treatment of hypertension, diabetic nephropathy, and congestive heart failure. Adverse effects include cough, hypotension, acute kidney injury, hyperkalemia, and angioedema. This patient has hypokalemia, making hydrochlorothiazide-induced hyponatremia the more likely cause of her hyponatremia and altered mental status. Adverse effect of lovastatin therapy (Choice C) is incorrect. Statins are used in the treatment of hyperlipidemia, particularly in patients with clinical signs or symptoms of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. A common side effect of statins is myopathy, which can present with fatigue and muscle weakness. However, statins do not cause hyponatremia and are unlikely to cause altered mental status. A cerebral infarction (Choice D) is caused by occlusion of a cerebral artery and presents with focal neurologic symptoms, such as hemiparesis or aphasia. It would be unlikely to present solely with mental status changes, particularly in the absence of changes on a CT scan of the head. Dementia, Alzheimer type (Choice E), presents with impairments in language, memory, perception, motor skills, attention, orientation, problem-solving, and executive function. Lethargy would be uncommon early in the disease, and this patient's electrolyte abnormalities are more likely contributing to her altered mental status than a primary neurologic disease, making an adverse effect of hydrochlorothiazide the most likely cause of her change in mental status.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 2, has had episodes of anxiety, bloating, fatigue, irritability, and breast tenderness for the past 2 years. Her symptoms begin about 10 days before menses and cease with the onset of menses. Physical examination is within normal limits. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in patient care?

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Instruction to keep a diary of symptoms for the next two menstrual cycles

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Measurement of serum thyroxine (T4), thyroid-stimulating hormone, and prolactin concentrations

questions.educationalObjective: Premenstrual dysphoric disorder consists of several affective and somatic symptoms occurring in the week before menses and resolving with the onset of menses. The diagnosis is established by the patient's prospective documentation of symptoms in relation to at least two menstrual cycles. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD) consists of affective and somatic symptoms occurring in the week before menses. According to the DSM-5, patients must demonstrate at least five symptoms. The symptoms must include at least one mood symptom (affective lability, marked irritability, depressed mood, or anxiety) and at least one of the following symptoms: anhedonia, concentration difficulties, decreased energy, hypersomnia or insomnia, increased or decreased appetite, a sense of being overwhelmed, and physical symptoms (eg, breast tenderness, muscle or joint pain, sensation of bloating, weight gain). The symptoms must begin to improve within a few days after menses onset and resolve during the week after menses. The diagnosis is established by the patient's prospective documentation of symptoms in relation to at least two menstrual cycles. The pathogenesis is likely related to modulation of neurotransmitters such as serotonin secondary to changes in estrogen and progesterone concentrations. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors are indicated in patients with moderate to severe PMDD, whereas patients with mild PMDD may benefit from counseling about stress reduction and lifestyle changes.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Reassurance and reexamination in 6 months (Choice A) would be inappropriate in this patient who is likely to have a PMDD diagnosis and may benefit from active management with medications or stress reduction techniques. Behavior modification therapy (Choice C) emphasizes reinforcement and punishment of certain behaviors. It is commonly utilized in autism spectrum disorder and attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder. However, this patient is not demonstrating problematic behaviors that require modification. Measurement of serum thyroxine (T4), thyroid-stimulating hormone, and prolactin concentrations (Choice D) are unnecessary in patients who meet diagnostic criteria for PMDD. Mood changes related to thyroid dysfunction are accompanied by other physical examination signs of thyroid disease (eg, skin and hair changes) and are not typically episodic. questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her daughter 2 hours after she fell at home. Physical examination shows ecchymoses over the left hip and left shoulder but is otherwise normal. During evaluation, the daughter pulls the physician aside and explains that her mother has drunk excessively over the past 12 years. She frequently consumes several bottles of wine alone at home each day. She also reports that her mother has been forgetting to take cooking pots off the stove. When the physician continues with the evaluation, the patient behaves as if she has never met the physician before now. On cognitive examination, she recalls zero out of three objects after 5 minutes. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's cognitive decline?
questions.educationalObjective: Chronic alcohol abuse and associated malnutrition may result in significant thiamine deficiency, which can lead to Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome. Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome is an irreversible condition that features anterograde and retrograde amnesia along with the consequent tendency to confabulate, or verbalize false stories or memories. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Patients who chronically abuse alcohol typically demonstrate a significant thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency caused by poor nutritional intake, thiamine malabsorption, and impaired cellular utilization of thiamine. Over time, thiamine deficiency can result in damage and atrophy of the mammillary bodies, visible on MRI of the brain, which manifests as Wernicke encephalopathy. The typical clinical presentation of Wernicke encephalopathy is a triad of acute altered mental status, ophthalmoplegia/nystagmus, and ataxia. The symptoms of Wernicke encephalopathy are reversible but increase the risk for developing Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome (also known as Korsakoff psychosis) in chronic alcohol users if not immediately treated with high-dose thiamine. Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome is an irreversible condition that features anterograde and retrograde amnesia along with the consequent tendency to confabulate, or verbalize false stories or memories, to compensate for the inability to remember. This patient demonstrates an inability to form new memories after the amnesia-causing event (anterograde amnesia).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (Choice A) is a human prion disease that presents with rapidly progressing dementia, neuropsychiatric symptoms, cerebellar dysfunction, corticospinal tract dysfunction, basal ganglia dysfunction, and stimulus-induced myoclonus. This patient does not demonstrate stimulus-induced myoclonus or cerebellar, corticospinal, or basal ganglia dysfunction. Dementia, Alzheimer type (Choice B), is the most prevalent dementia, presenting with progressive cognitive decline that begins with short-term memory impairment, progresses to apraxia and language abnormalities, and culminates in behavioral and personality changes preventing the patient from performing basic activities of daily living. Anterograde memory impairment is common, but this patient's young age is inconsistent with dementia, Alzheimer type. Lab Values Calculator Correct Answer: C. Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (Choice A) is a human prion disease that presents with rapidly progressing dementia, neuropsychiatric symptoms, cerebellar dysfunction, corticospinal tract dysfunction, basal ganglia dysfunction, and stimulus-induced myoclonus. This patient does not demonstrate stimulus-induced myoclonus or cerebellar, corticospinal, or basal ganglia dysfunction. Dementia, Alzheimer type (Choice B), is the most prevalent dementia, presenting with progressive cognitive decline that begins with short-term memory impairment, progresses to apraxia and language abnormalities, and culminates in behavioral and personality changes preventing the patient from performing basic activities of daily living. Anterograde memory impairment is common, but this patient's young age is inconsistent with dementia, Alzheimer type. Multi-infarct (vascular) dementia (Choice D) typically presents with stepwise declines in cognition along with focal neurologic deficits and commonly occurs in patients with cardiovascular risk factors. This patient does not demonstrate focal neurologic deficits on examination or possess a history of cardiovascular risk factors. Wernicke encephalopathy (Choice E) classically presents with a triad of acutely altered mental status, ophthalmoplegia/nystagmus, and ataxia. This patient does not demonstrate ophthalmoplegia or ataxia on examination (though ataxia may have been the cause of her fall). On examination, the patient demonstrates anterograde amnesia, indicating that she has progressed to Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her father as a new patient for a well-child examination. On entering the physician's office, the patient immediately climbs onto the physician's lap and begins talking with the physician. Her father says that his daughter has shown poor learning ability and has outbursts in school. He also says she is highly sensitive to sound. Examination shows a small, upturned nose, small chin, and long philtrum. Muscle tone is decreased. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: As a result of a deletion on chromosome 7q11.23, patients with Williams syndrome typically present with an overly friendly demeanor, emotional dysregulation, intellectual delay, elfin facies, short stature, vascular and cardiac valve abnormalities secondary to elastin dysfunction, structural abnormalities of the urinary tract, and endocrine abnormalities. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Williams syndrome results from a sporadic or inherited deletion of contiguous genes on chromosome 7q11.23, including the elastin gene. Patients typically present with an overly friendly demeanor, emotional dysregulation, intellectual delay, elfin facies (strabismus, flat nasal bridge, short nose, long philtrum, wide mouth), short stature, vascular and cardiac valve abnormalities secondary to elastin dysfunction, structural abnormalities of the urinary tract, and endocrine abnormalities (eg, hypothyroidism). Diagnosis is confirmed by genetic testing confirming a deletion on chromosome 7q 11.23. !Management involves surgical correction of vascular, cardiac, or other functionally significant structural abnormalities and antihypertensive medication if such develops.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Cornelia de Lange syndrome (Choice A) results from the sporadic deletion of cohesin complex genes, which normally direct prenatal development. It is characterized by growth delay, intellectual disability, facial dysmorphia (microcephaly, arched eyebrows, small upturned nose, widely spaced teeth), and malformations of the extremities. Down syndrome (Choice B) results from trisomy of chromosome 21 and presents with characteristic physical features including broad, flat, facial features with prominent epicanthal folds and a single palmar crease. Patients with Down syndrome also present with cognitive impairment, cardiac abnormalities (eg, atrial septal defect), gastrointestinal abnormalities (eg, duodenal atresia, imperforate anus), ophthalmologic abnormalities (eg, refractive errors), and hearing loss. Prader-Willi syndrome (Choice C) results from the absence of paternal expression of genes on chromosome 15. Children typically present with hyperphagia (and potential obesity), short stature, impaired cognition, motor delays, and hypogonadism. questions.retrievalTag: Discussion of her preferences for care
questions.stem: A 77-year-old woman comes to the physician at the request of an attorney prior to writing an advance directive. She says that she wants to have an advance directive in order to avoid putting an unnecessary burden on her children. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She immigrated to the USA from Poland 20 years ago. She has only an elementary school education. She was employed in a cleaning service for a hotel but stopped working 5 years ago. She lives alone and is able to perform activities of daily living independently. She participates in social activities at her church. All of her close friends are also from Poland. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she is nervous. She is alert and is oriented to person. She knows she is in the physician's office but does not recall the street name correctly. She knows the month and day of the week but is not sure of the exact date. She knows her address. She can recall two of three words after 5 minutes. She can subtract 7 from 100 using her fingers but is unable to subtract 7 from 93. She is unable to spell the word "world" backwards. She can draw the hands on a clock to show 10 minutes to 2 o'clock and can slowly copy a drawing of two intersecting pentagons. She demonstrates that she understands the meaning of a standardized story. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step with respect to this patient's advance directive?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Discussion of her preferences for care

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Repeat her mental status examination with an interpreter

questions.educationalObjective: To meet the criteria for decisional capacity, patients must express a consistent choice, demonstrate an understanding of the risks and benefits of the choice, demonstrate an appreciation of the significance of the choice, and illustrate an ability to reason through the options. Regardless of a patient's cognitive ability, physicians should assess patients' decisional capacity for each specific care decision. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Though some cognitively impaired patients may lack capacity to make decisions about their care, cognitively impaired patients should not be assumed to lack decisional capacity. Though this patient illustrates mild abnormalities on brief cognitive testing (which may indicate normal aging changes in a patient with a limited education), these findings do not necessarily preclude her from making end-of- life decisions. To meet the criteria for decisional capacity, patients must express a consistent choice, demonstrate an understanding of the risks and benefits of the choice, demonstrate an appreciation of the significance of the choice for their personal circumstances, and illustrate an ability to reason through the options. Therefore, to assess this patient's decisional capacity and potentially write her advance directive (if the physician believes the patient possesses decisional capacity), the physician should discuss the patient's preferences for care and assess the four decisional capacity criteria specific to these decisions.

questions.stem: Six hours after undergoing an appendectomy, a 47-year-old man reports a moderately severe, diffuse headache. He has no other symptoms. He has not ingested food or drink for 2 days. His pulse is 90/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Neurologic examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Caffeine-withdrawal headaches are typically diffuse and pulsating, typically occurring within a day after discontinuing caffeine use. Other symptoms of caffeine withdrawal include fatigue, irritable mood, difficulty concentrating, tachycardia, tremor, gastrointestinal upset, and flu- like symptoms. Lab Values Calculator

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Headaches can be differentiated into primary and secondary headaches (eg, substances, infection, neoplasm, trauma). Headache features that raise concern for serious secondary causes include fever, focal neurologic deficits, abrupt onset, history of malignancy or trauma, and positional exacerbation. Caffeine withdrawal typically presents with headache, fatigue, depressed or irritable mood, difficulty concentrating, gastrointestinal distress, tachycardia, hand tremor, or flu-like symptoms. The headache is commonly diffuse and pulsating, varies from mild to severe in intensity, begins within 24 hours of last caffeine consumption, and improves once withdrawal resolves. Acute management may include over-the-counter analgesics or the administration of caffeine. Patients desiring to abstain from caffeine should gradually taper their use to prevent severe withdrawal symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers

This patient does not demonstrate a fever or signs of meningeal irritation other than headache. Brain abscess (Choice B) typically presents with fever, focal neurologic deficits, headache, and altered mental status. This patient does not demonstrate a fever, focal neurologic deficits, or altered mental status. Cluster headaches (Choice D) are primary headaches that typically present with short-lived, unilateral, severe pain in the orbital, supraorbital, or temporal region of the head and are accompanied by ipsilateral autonomic symptoms (eg, miosis, ptosis, lacrimation, nasal congestion). This patient endorses a diffuse headache and does not demonstrate autonomic symptoms. questions.stem: A 67-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 1-year history of difficulty sitting still for more than 1 hour. He says that he has a nearly intolerable sensation of restlessness in his legs when watching movies or during long flights. His symptoms resolve when he stands up and takes a few steps. He also has had difficulty falling asleep because of his symptoms and states that he thinks he moves around a lot once he falls asleep because his wife often complains that he kicks her in bed. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Deep tendon reflexes are normal in all extremities. Sensation is intact. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy?
questions.educationalObjective: Restless legs syndrome (RLS) is a common movement disorder characterized by an urge to move the lower extremities when lying down to sleep. During sleep, many patients with RLS demonstrate periodic movements of the lower extremities. Lab Values Calculator because his wife often complains that he kicks her in bed. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Deep tendon reflexes are normal in all extremities. Sensation is intact. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy? A) B) C) D) E) Hydroxyzine Modafinil Ropinirole Trazodone Zolpidem Correct Answer: C. Restless legs syndrome (RLS) is a common movement disorder characterized by an urge to move the lower extremities when sitting or lying down to sleep. During sleep, many patients with RLS demonstrate periodic movements of the lower extremities. The pathogenesis is incompletely understood, but alterations in dopamine metabolism and cellular iron influx and efflux may be involved. Caffeine along with antihistamine, dopamine antagonist, and antidepressant medications commonly exacerbate RLS. All patients with RLS should undergo iron studies. Patients with iron deficiency require iron repletion. In patients without iron deficiency who report chronic and severe symptoms, dopamine agonists such as ropinirole are indicated. Incorrect Answers: A, B, D, and E. Hydroxyzine (Choice A) is an antihistamine medication utilized for allergic reactions and off-label for anxiety. It commonly exacerbates RLS. Modafinil (Choice B) is commonly utilized to address daytime sleepiness in patients with narcolepsy, obstructive sleep apnea, and shift- work sleep disorder. It has no proven efficacy in RLS. Trazodone (Choice D) is a serotonin modulator, and zolpidem (Choice E) is a nonbenzodiazepine sedative-hypnotic medication. Both are utilized to treat insomnia but are not helpful in RLS. questions.stem: A 37-year-old woman comes to the physician because her husband is concerned that she has been having difficulty remembering things around the house. During the past 6 weeks, she has been forgetting to do her usual household chores. She does not think that she has a problem. She says that she does her best but often feels fatigued, especially when moving around a lot. She has had numbness of both legs and intermittent blurred vision during the past year. Physical examination shows pallor of the optic discs. Deep tendon reflexes at the knees and ankles are brisk bilaterally. On mental status examination, she has an upbeat mood and affect. She is oriented to person, place, and time. She has difficulty remembering the names of recent U.S. Presidents. There is no evidence of auditory or visual hallucinations. A T2-weighted MRI of the brain shows numerous small plaques in the periventricular white matter. A lumbar puncture is done. Which of the following findings is most likely on cerebrospinal fluid analysis?
questions.educationalObjective: Multiple sclerosis is an autoimmune demyelinating disease that presents with focal neurologic deficits separated in space and time. Fatigue and exercise-induced symptom exacerbation are common. The diagnosis is supported by oligoclonal bands of immunoglobulins in the cerebrospinal fluid, indicating immune overactivation. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Multiple sclerosis, the most prevalent immune-mediated demyelinating disease, can present initially with lesions in several central nervous system (CNS) locations. According to the McDonald criteria for multiple sclerosis diagnosis, patients must have evidence of demyelinating lesions that are separated in time and location. This evidence can be based on subjective symptoms or imaging evidence. This patient demonstrated several episodes of optic neuritis separated in time, along with dysfunction of the dorsal column- medial lemniscus tracts (decreased sensation), corticospinal tracts (hyperreflexia), frontal lobes (incongruently bright affect), and medial temporal lobe (decreased recall memory), representing separation in space. Fatigue and symptom exacerbation with exercise are common in multiple sclerosis. MRI of the brain typically shows focal white matter hyperintensities in the periventricular region, cortex, subcortical region, cerebellum, or spine. Oligoclonal bands of immunoglobulins, indicating immune overactivation, can be found in the cerebrospinal fluid but are not necessary for diagnosis. Treatment includes corticosteroids for symptomatic management of acute flares and long-term disease-modifying treatments such as interferon or monoclonal antibodies, which decrease the frequency of future episodes.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Decreased glucose (Choice A) is demonstrated in bacterial and fungal meningitis or encephalitis. Eosinophilocytosis (Choice C) is consistent with eosinophilic meningitis secondary to parasitic infections. This patient's focal neurologic deficits and chronic course along with the absence of headache, encephalopathy (eg, decreased alertness, disorientation), or systemic signs of infection are more consistent with multiple sclerosis. Lab Values Calculator E) No abnormalities Correct Answer: D. Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, and E. Decreased glucose (Choice A) is demonstrated in bacterial and fungal meningitis or encephalitis. Eosinophilocytosis (Choice C) is consistent with eosinophilic meningitis secondary to parasitic infections. This patient's focal neurologic deficits and chronic course along with the absence of headache, encephalopathy (eg, decreased alertness, disorientation), or systemic signs of infection are more consistent with multiple sclerosis. Decreased total protein concentration (Choice B) may occur in patients with increased intracerebral pressure (caused by efflux of protein into the arachnoid granulations), water intoxication, or hyperthyroidism. However, this patient does not demonstrate papilledema or other symptoms of increased intracerebral pressure (eg, nausea, vomiting, positional headaches). Also, she does not illustrate physical examination signs of hyperthyroidism or cerebral edema consistent with water intoxication. No abnormalities of the cerebrospinal fluid analysis (Choice E) would be atypical of multiple sclerosis, which often demonstrates oligoclonal bands.

questions.stem: A 57-year-old lawyer is brought to the emergency department by police after he interrupted a church service by loudly proclaiming that he is the Messiah. Police say that he became angry and physically confrontational when he was asked to leave and continued to insist that he was the Messiah when police intervened. On arrival in the emergency department, he is expansive and talks loudly. He appears well groomed. On questioning, he says he has never been sick and takes no medications. He does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs. He refuses to allow a physical examination and becomes irritable with the nurses. He demands to see the person in charge of the hospital, noting he has figured out a way to save the hospital millions of dollars. On mental status examination, he talks rapidly and abruptly shifts from topic to topic. He reports hearing the voice of God telling him that He is pleased with him. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Bipolar disorder is a mood disorder that features acute episodes of hypomania or mania and may include episodes of depression. Acute mania typically manifests with an irritable or euphoric mood, increased energy, a decreased need for sleep, increased goal-directed activity, rapid speech and thinking, irresponsibility, and grandiose thinking. Psychotic features (eg, hallucinations, delusions) may also occur. Lab Values Calculator

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Bipolar disorder is a mood disorder that features acute episode,s of mania. This patient's career as a lawyer indicates he is high functioning at baseline, signifying that his current behavior is an episodic change from baseline. Acute mania typically manifests with an expansive or euphoric or irritable mood, increased energy, a decreased need for sleep, increased goal-directed activity, rapid speech and thinking (as evidenced by this patient's pressured speech with a flight of ideas), irresponsibility (as evidenced by this patient's disruption of a church service), and grandiose thinking (as evidenced by this patient's confidence that his plan can save the hospital millions of dollars). Some patients demonstrate associated psychotic symptoms (eg, hallucinations, delusions). This patient's belief that he is the Messiah constitutes a grandiose delusion. Type 1 bipolar disorder is this patient's most likely diagnosis. Though patients with type 1 bipolar disorder need to demonstrate a history of mania to meet diagnostic criteria, patients with type 2 bipolar disorder must demonstrate major depressive episodes in addition to hypomanic episodes (less severe and shorter episodes of mania without psychotic features). Management requires mood stabilizing therapy (eg, mood stabilizers or mood-stabilizing antipsychotics) for acute mania. Once acute mood episodes resolve, patients with bipolar disorder should continue mood stabilizer maintenance therapy to prevent future episodes of mania or depression.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers

Incorrect Answers: B, C, D, and E Delusional disorder (Choice B) is characterized by the presence of one or more delusions for a month or longer without other psychotic symptoms. Mania symptoms are atypical. Dysthymic disorder (Choice C), more commonly known as persistent depressive disorder, refers to depression symptoms lasting longer than two years. Mania symptoms and an episodic course are atypical. Schizophrenia (Choice D) is characterized by a chronic course of delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, and/or negative symptoms (eg, flat affect, apathy, alogia) outside of manic or depressive episodes. This patient's hallucinations and grandiose delusions are secondary to acute mania. This patient's episodic course without evidence of residual symptoms or impaired functionality between episodes is also consistent with a mood disorder rather than a chronic psychotic disorder. Schizotypal personality disorder (Choice E) is a cluster A personality disorder, the odd or eccentric cluster. The disorder is characterized by odd or magical behavior and thinking and a constricted affect. Mania symptoms are atypical. This patient's episodic course without evidence of residual dysfunction between episodes is also consistent with a mood disorder rather than a personality disorder.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 2 hours after the onset of chest pain, palpitations, and agitation. He has a history of cocaine abuse and has been treated in the emergency department six times during the past 4 months for episodes of agitation and hallucinations. He has no other history of serious illness and takes no medications. On arrival, he is agitated but cooperative. His temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), pulse is 130/min, and blood pressure is 160/100 mm Hg. Examination shows no other abnormalities. Urine toxicology screening is positive for cocaine. An ECG shows new ST-segment depressions in the anterior leads, which were not found on ECGs obtained at previous visits. Which of the following medications is contraindicated in this patient?
questions.educationalObjective: Cocaine is a sympathomimetic medication that leads to increased cardiac chronotropy and peripheral vasoconstriction. It can also cause coronary artery vasospasm with subsequent myocardial ischemia. -Adrenergic blocker monotherapy is contraindicated in patients who use cocaine, as it can lead to unopposed a1-adrenergic activation, which presents with marked hypertension and further increases the risk for coronary artery vasospasm. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Cocaine is an addictive recreational drug that causes euphoria, restlessness, and increased sympathetic tone (tachycardia, hypertension, pupillary dilation). Cocaine blocks the presynaptic reuptake of norepinephrine, serotonin, and dopamine, which increases the concentration of these substances in the synaptic cleft. The increased norepinephrine causes tachycardia and hypertension through activation of sympathetic adrenoceptors, leading to increased cardiac chronotropy and peripheral vasoconstriction. It can also cause coronary artery vasospasm, which presents with chest pain and new ECG changes (eg, T wave inversions, ST elevations or depressions). -Adrenergic blocker therapy in cocaine intoxication can lead to unopposed a1-adrenergic activation, which can lead to marked hypertension and further increase the risk for coronary vasospasm. Therefore, in this patient, administering -adrenergic blocker monotherapy with metoprolol would be contraindicated.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers

It should be given as soon as possible when a patient presents with signs and symptoms of myocardial ischemia, such as chest pain, diaphoresis, nausea, and ECG changes. Aspirin would be appropriate in this patient and is not contraindicated in cocaine intoxication. Diazepam (Choice B) is a benzodiazepine that is used in the treatment of anxiety, seizures, and alcohol detoxification. It can also be used to decrease the agitation associated with cocaine intoxication and is indicated in this patient. questions.stem: A 37-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 4-month history of recurrent thoughts that she has sexually abused her 5-year-old daughter. She says that she examines her daughter's perineum several times daily looking for evidence of abuse. Examination of the child by her pediatrician has shown no evidence of abuse. The patient believes that she is going crazy. She reports that she was sexually abused as a child. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she appears tense, anxious, and mildly depressed. She cries as she describes her worrisome thoughts. There is no evidence of hallucinations. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: OCD is an anxiety disorder typically characterized by obsessions (unwanted, intrusive thoughts that produce anxiety) that the patient attempts to neutralize with compulsions (repetitive behaviors). By definition, the compulsions are excessive or do not realistically address the obsessive fears. Lab Values Calculator

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Obsessive-compulsive disorderr (OCD) is an anxiety disorder typically characterized by obsessions that the patient attempts to neutralize with compulsions. Obsessions are unwanted, intrusive thoughts (eg, thoughts related to violence, sex, or contamination) or urges (eg, to commit violence) that increase anxiety. Compulsions are rigidly applied repetitive behaviors (eg, checking, washing) or mental activities (eg, counting, repeating words) that relieve anxiety related to obsessions. By definition, the compulsions are excessive or do not realistically address the obsessive fears, and patients commonly recognize the irrationality of their compulsions. The pathogenesis of OCD is likely multifactorial, related to genetics, stress, or dysfunction of striatal and thalamic circuits. Treatment includes high doses of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRls, eg, fluoxetine) or cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) with a focus on exposure to the discomfort of suppressing the compulsion after experiencing the obsession.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Acute stress disorder (Choice A) refers to intrusive thoughts, hyperarousal, avoidance, or negative mood and thoughts that occur within 1 month of a traumatic event such as a life-threatening motor vehicle collision. This patient has no history of a traumatic event within the past month. Delusional disorder (Choice B) is characterized by the presence of one or more delusions for a month or longer without other psychotic symptoms. Patients typically lack insight into their delusion, which they accept as truth. As such, delusional thoughts are usually ego- syntonic and nondistressing. This patient is instead experiencing distressing, intrusive, ego-dystonic thoughts that she is likely able to identify as untrue . Lab Values Calculator Correct Answer: E. questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the physician for a well-child examination. His mother states that he has had increasingly impulsive behavior and aggressive outbursts during the past year. His kindergarten and first grade teachers expressed concern about his ability to follow directions and socialize with peers. At the age of 3 years, he received treatment for lead exposure. Language development is appropriate; toilet training milestones have been met. His mother states that he is a picky eater. He has no history of serious illness. His mother believes that he is angry about his parents getting a divorce. He is alert and fidgety. He is at the 5th percentile for height and weight. His pulse is 78/min, and blood pressure is 97/60 mm Hg. Examination shows no dysmorphic features. The thyroid gland is not enlarged. Pulmonary and abdominal examinations show no abnormalities. The upper and lower extremities are thin. Muscle tone is normal. Deep tendon reflexes are brisk. He is easily distractible, impulsive in observed play, and oppositional when his mother attempts to redirect him. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: ADHD presents with chronic symptoms of hyperactivity/impulsivity or inattention that occur in more than one setting and impair academic, social, or emotional function. Diagnosis requires that parents and teachers fill out behavioral rating scales. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) presents with chronic symptoms of hyperactivity/impulsivity or inattention that occur in more than one setting and impair academic, social, or emotional function. Children with predominant hyperactivity symptoms are unable to sit still and may have difficulty taking turns (postulated to result from a dysregulated dopamine reward pathway), whereas children with predominant inattentive symptoms tend to daydream, process information slowly, and demonstrate difficulty in completing tasks (related to norepinephrine dysregulation). ADHD is associated with soft neurologic abnormalities such as brief reflexes. For diagnosis, physicians must establish that the patient is functionally impaired in several settings, so parents and teachers must fill out behavioral rating scales. Stimulants, which include amphetamine salts and methylphenidate, are first-line agents for treatment that increase synaptic dopamine and norepinephrine.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Projective testing by the school psychologist (Choice B) would involve the patient interpreting ambiguous stimuli (eg, Rorschach testing) as a personality test. This patient does not demonstrate a pathologic personality and this testing is inappropriate at this time. Thyroid function tests (Choice C) would assist in diagnosing thyroid dysfunction, which may present with hyperactivity. However, this patient does not demonstrate tachycardia or physical examination signs of thyroid dysfunction (eg ,skin, hair, or eye changes). \ j Lab Values Calculator B) Projective testing by school psychologist C) Thyroid function tests D) MRI of the brain E) EEG Correct Answer: A. questions.stem: A 15-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by paramedics 30 minutes after his friends noticed that he had become confused and unsteady. His friends reported that he "may have been trying to get high." Glucose and naloxone administered en route to the emergency department produced no change in his condition. On arrival, he is lethargic, and his speech is slurred. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 150/95 mm Hg. His gait is ataxic. Results of urine toxicology screening are pending. A CT scan of the head shows no abnormalities. The patient falls asleep for 1 hour, and a second neurologic examination on awakening shows full alertness and a normal gait. Which of the following substances is the most likely cause of these findings?
questions.educationalObjective: Inhalant abuse commonly manifests with neuropsychiatric symptoms (eg, headache, ataxia, anosmia, slurred speech, mood swings, hallucinations). The effects typically begin within seconds of use and persist for approximately 30 minutes, representing a faster onset and resolution compared with other recreational drugs. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Inhalant abuse (eg, toluene, nitrous oxide, spray paint, glue) is common in children and adolescents. The effects typically begin within seconds of use and persist for approximately 30 minutes, representing a faster onset and resolution compared with other recreational drugs. Inhalants exert effects on multiple organ systems: dermatologic (eg, perioral or perinasal dermatitis with erythro- or pyoderma), otolaryngologic (eg, nosebleeds, halitosis), ocular (eg, conjunctiva! infection), cardiac (eg, dysrhythmia, tachycardia), gastrointestinal (eg, nausea, anorexia), respiratory (eg, wheezing, coughing, sneezing), and neuropsychiatric (eg, headache, ataxia, anosmia, slurred speech, mood swings, hallucinations). Severe inhalant toxicity can manifest with tachydysrhythmias and respiratory depression, and ultimately seizures or coma. Treatment is primarily supportive, though some inhalants have specific antidotes (eg, methylene blue for nitrites). Prevention is key; schools should monitor the use of solvent-based products, and parents and children should be educated about the risks of inhalants.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Alcohol (Choice A) is a central nervous system (CNS) depressant that can cause slurred speech, emotional lability, behavioral disinhibition, ataxia, and memory lapses. However, alcohol is typically metabolized over the course of hours, not minutes. Cocaine (Choice B) is a CNS stimulant that causes euphoria and increases sympathetic tone, leading to tachycardia and hypertension, pupillary dilation, and restlessness (rather than lethargy). Also, cocaine is typically metabolized over hours, not minutes. Heroin (Choice C) causes symptoms of CNS depression including sedation, respiratory depression, bradycardia, and hypotension. However, heroin is typically metabolized over the course of hours, not minutes. questions.stem: A 20-year-old woman comes to the physician with her husband because of increasing fatigue and social withdrawal. The patient reports that she has always worried about her appearance and how other people perceive her. She has difficulty shopping and states, "Every time I go into a grocery store, I feel like I might not get out alive." During the past 3 months, she has induced vomiting 6 to 10 times weekly when she feels "panicky" after eating "bad foods." Her husband reports that she becomes anxious and irritable when she has not exercised. She has not had a menstrual period for 6 months. Her husband expresses frustration with her problems and says that her obsessive worrying and poor self-image are ruining their marriage. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She is thin and meticulously groomed. She is 165 cm (5 ft 5 in) tall and weighs 48 kg (105 lb); BMI is 18 kg/m2. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 50/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 102/68 mm Hg. Physical examination, including pelvic examination, shows no abnormalities. The patient states that her recent weight loss of 6.8 kg (15 lb) has made her feel less anxious and that she plans to lose more weight. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Anorexia nervosa is characterized by an intense fear of gaining weight, decreased self-worth related to body weight, and consequent restricting (eg, fasting, exercising) or binge eating/purging behavior (eg, vomiting, laxative or diuretic misuse) leading to insufficient energy intake. Amenorrhea and a low BMI are more consistent with anorexia nervosa than bulimia nervosa. Lab Values Calculator

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Anorexia nervosa is characterized by an intense fear of gaining weight, decreased self-worth related to body weight, and consequent restricting (eg, fasting, exercising) or binge eating/purging behavior (eg, vomiting, laxative or diuretic misuse) leading to insufficient nutrition. Patients with anorexia nervosa often have a BMI less than 17 kg/m2, though patients with mild anorexia may demonstrate a BMI greater than 17 kg/m2(normal BMI is 18.5 to 24.9 kg/m2). Amenorrhea is common and reflects insufficient nutrition. Alternatively, patients with bulimia nervosa do not typically demonstrate severe malnutrition that leads to amenorrhea or a low BMI. Physical examination of patients with anorexia nervosa can show dry, scaly skin and fine hair or hair loss caused by decreased protein stores. Patients who vomit to purge can demonstrate parotid gland enlargement from salivary gland overstimulation, dental caries from gastric hydrochloric acid erosion of enamel, and scars on the knuckles secondary to abrasions from the incisors when inducing vomiting. The loss of gastric hydrochloric acid leads to hypochloremia and metabolic alkalosis. In severe cases, signs of hypovolemia such as tachycardia and hypotension may be present. Treatment of anorexia nervosa is through a combined medical and psychiatric approach and involves correcting fluid and electrolyte derangements along with behavioral and pharmacologic therapy.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers

Bulimia nervosa (Choice C) involves cycles of uncontrollable eating and compensatory behaviors such as fasting, exercising, vomiting, laxative overuse, or diuretic overuse. Insufficient nutrition with consequent amenorrhea and low BMI is more consistent with anorexia nervosa. Generalized anxiety disorder (Choice D) is an anxiety disorder that features excessive worrying about a range of topics with associated functional impairment. This patient's worries are specific to food and body weight. Obsessive-compulsive disorderr (Choice E) is an anxiety disorder typically characterized by obsessions (unwanted, intrusive thoughts that produce anxiety) that the patient attempts to neutralize with compulsions (repetitive behaviors). A ritual involving compulsive vomiting is more consistent with an eating disorder such as anorexia nervosa. While the compulsions of patients with obsessive- compulsive disorder are motivated by relieving distress from obsessions, patients with eating disorders are motivated by body image, as manifested in this patient's poor oral intake combined with h,er vomiting behaviors.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her mother because of frequent temper tantrums and extreme irritability over the past 6 months. She was born at 35 weeks' gestation and had jaundice at birth. Her 17-year-old mother reports that her daughter refuses to listen to her and will not eat foods she previously ate. The mother appears sad and states that she is overwhelmed. The patient is at the 15th percentile for height and weight. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. She is very active during the examination. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answerChoices.label: D

questions.educationalObjective: Typical preschoolers demonstrate defiant behavior and are picky eaters. Teenage parents may be ill-equipped to respond to challenging behaviors and commonly benefit from parenting classes. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Normal 4-year-old children play with other children, play make-believe, tell stories, understand time and counting, stand and hop on one foot, and cut their own food with supervision. Typical preschool-age children also demonstrate defiant behavior and are picky eaters. When children demonstrate difficult behaviors, parents should utilize ignoring and other parenting skills. Teenage parents commonly possess inadequate parenting skills and may increase their attention to the child during tantrums, reinforcing the tantrum behavior. Young parents typically benefit from parenting classes.

questions.retrievalTag: Major depressive disorder with atypical features
questions.stem: A previously healthy 57-year-old woman comes to the physician because of memory loss for 3 months. Examination shows a very sluggish patient with a flat affect who talks slowly and in a whisper. Initially, she replies to all mental status tests, "I don't know" or "I can't remember." As the physician shows a warm and supportive manner, her responses improve, so that eventually she recalls the main event in the news the previous evening and is able to recall two out of three objects after 5 minutes. She is told the third object. When retested just before leaving 10 minutes later, she recalls all three. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: In patients with major depressive disorder (MDD) with atypical features, neurovegetative symptoms typically Lab Values Calculator (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance). These symptoms disrupt everyday functioning. In patients with MDD with atypical features, neurovegetative symptoms typically manifest as hypersomnia and hyperphagia. Patients with MDD with atypical features may also demonstrate situational improvements in mood, oversensitivity to interpersonal rejection, or leaden paralysis (a heavy sensation in the extremities leading to extreme psychomotor retardation). The underlying pathogenesis is believed to arise from personality and temperament characteristics that promote emotional and behavioral dysregulation in response to stressful situations such as interpersonal rejection. This patient's subjective memory loss and severe psychomotor retardation that improve as she develops a rapport with the physician likely represents a functional neurologic disorder (reflecting the conversion of emotional distress into neurologic symptoms), which is frequently comorbid with depression. When the patient's fear of interpersonal rejection by the physician decreases, her distress and consequent psychological need to convert her distress into a neurologic symptom dissipates. Treatment of atypical depressive disorder involves antidepressant medication and psychotherapy. Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, D, E, and G. Communicating hydrocephalus (Choice A) refers to hydrocephalus secondary to inadequate reabsorption of cerebrospinal fluid. Patients typically present with positional headaches, nausea, vomiting, and vision changes (signs of increased intracranial pressure). Memory loss in the absence of other hydrocephalus symptoms that improves over the course of minutes is atypical. Concussion (Choice B), or mild traumatic brain injury, manifests as a loss of consciousness, confusion, amnesia, headache, dizziness, nausea, or vomiting. Memory loss in the absence of other concussion symptoms that improves over the course of minutes is atypical. Dementia, Alzheimer type (Choice C), is the most prevalent dementia, presenting with progressive cognitive decline that culminates in the inability to perform basic activities of daily living. As dementia, Alzheimer type, is a neurodegenerative disease, significant memory improvement is atypical. Herpes simplex encephalitis (Choice D) commonly presents with fever, headache, seizures, focal neurologic signs, and confusion. Apart from fluctuating memory loss, this patient does not demonstrate symptoms of herpes simplex encephalitis. Hysterical amnesia (Choice E), also known as dissociative amnesia, refers to the inability to recall traumatic or anxiety-inducing events secondary to psychological distress. This patient's initial cognitive impairment is generalized rather than specific to a traumatic event. Transient global amnesia (Choice G) refers to a sudden loss of anterograde memory and disorientation that lasts up to 24 hours. This patient's subjective memory loss has endured 3 months. Educational Objective: In patients with major depressive disorder (MDD) with atypical features, neurovegetative symptoms typically manifest as hypersomnia and hyperphagia. Patients also present with situational improvements in mood, oversensitivity to interpersonal rejection, or leaden paralysis. Physicians should suspect MDD with atypical features in patients whose depressive symptoms or cognitive concerns improve in certain situations (eg, with building interpersonal rapport). Lab Values Calculator

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Symptoms of major depressive disorder (MDD) include 2 or more weeks of at least five of the following symptoms: depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, psychomotor retardation, suicidal thoughts, or neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance). These symptoms disrupt everyday functioning. In patients with MDD with atypical features, neurovegetative symptoms typically manifest as hypersomnia and hyperphagia. Patients with MDD with atypical features may also demonstrate situational improvements in mood, oversensitivity to interpersonal rejection, or leaden paralysis (a heavy sensation in the extremities leading to extreme psychomotor retardation). The underlying pathogenesis is believed to arise from personality and temperament characteristics that promote emotional and behavioral dysregulation in response to stressful situations such as interpersonal rejection. This patient's subjective memory loss and severe psychomotor retardation that improve as she develops a rapport with the physician likely represents a functional neurologic disorder (reflecting the conversion of emotional distress into neurologic symptoms), which is frequently comorbid with depression. When the patient's fear of interpersonal rejection by the physician decreases, her distress and consequent psychological need to convert her distress into a neurologic symptom dissipates. Treatment of atypical depressive disorder involves antidepressant medication and psychotherapy.

questions.stem: A 21-year-old college student is brought to the emergency department by her roommates because of a 4-day history of progressive generalized weakness and muscle cramps. She has been unable to eat solids for 2 days. Her last bowel movement was 4 days ago. Her roommates report that, during the past year, they have heard her vomiting in the bathroom after meals. Her last menstrual period was 3 weeks ago. The patient is oriented to person, place, and time and says that she feels tired. Her temperature is 36°C (96.8°F), pulse is 70/min, and blood pressure is 100/70 mm Hg. The abdomen is soft, and bowel sounds are decreased. Muscle strength is decreased throughout. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Bulimia nervosa (binging/purging type) involves cycles of uncontrollable eating and compensatory behaviors such as vomiting, laxative, or diuretic overuse. Patients who regularly vomit may develop hypokalemia, which typically presents with fatigue, generalized weakness, and muscle cramping. Lab Values Calculator

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Bulimia nervosa (binging/purging type) involves cycles of uncontrollable eating and compensatory behaviors such as vomiting, laxative, or diuretic overuse that occur at least once a week for 3 months or more. Unlike patients with anorexia nervosa, patients with bulimia nervosa typically have a normal BMI and do not demonstrate amenorrhea. Patients with the binging/purging type of anorexia or bulimia nervosa can demonstrate dental caries from gastric hydrochloric acid erosion of enamel, parotid gland swelling from salivary gland overstimulation, and scars on the knuckles secondary to abrasions from the incisors when inducing vomiting. The loss of gastric hydrochloric acid leads to hypochloremia and metabolic alkalosis. Hypokalemia is common, as increased bicarbonate leads to renal proton-potassium exchange and consequent potassium secretion. In severe cases of bulimia nervosa, signs of hypovolemia such as tachycardia and hypotension may be present. Hypovolemia leads to increased aldosterone concentrations, resulting in further potassium secretion. As potassium is involved in the membrane potential of muscle cells (including skeletal, smooth, and cardiac muscles), hypokalemia typically leads to general weakness, muscle cramps, fatigue, palpitations, or decreased gastrointestinal motility. Treatment of bulimia nervosa is through a combined medical and psychiatric approach, and it involves correcting fluid and electrolyte derangements alongside behavioral and pharmacologic therapy.

questions.stem: A 23-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by police after he had to be removed in handcuffs from an airliner on which he had been a passenger. The airline attendants report that the patient shouted that he was a pilot and demanded to fly the plane, because he was too busy to wait for "those idiots that the airline calls pilots to take off." The patient reports, "I felt exquisite and perfectly glorious until those Keystone Cops started harassing me." He states that he has been brought to the hospital by "peons" in the past who failed to understand his essential superiority to "normal man." He says he has racing thoughts and boundless energy and has been sleeping only 2 hours nightly for the past week. He adds that he recently quit his job as a software engineer because he is now in complete control of the US government. He takes no medications. He has increased his alcohol consumption to six beers daily for the past 3 days. He does not use illicit drugs. He is wearing bright, mismatched clothing. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, his mood is expansive and irritable, and his speech is loud and rapid. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy?
questions.educationalObjective: Acute mania typically manifests with an irritable or euphoric mood, increased energy, a decreased need for sleep, increased goal-directed activity, rapid speech and thinking, irresponsibility, and grandiose thinking. Management requires mood stabilizer or atypical antipsychotic medications, which possess mood stabilizing properties and act rapidly. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Bipolar disorder is a mood disorder that features acute episodes of mania. Acute mania typically manifests with an irritable or euphoric mood, increased energy, a decreased need for sleep, increased goal-directed activity, rapid speech and thinking, irresponsibility (as evidenced by this patient's demand to fly the plane), and grandiose thinking. Some patients demonstrate associated psychotic symptoms (eg, grandiose delusions, hallucinations). Management of acute mania requires mood stabilizing therapy (eg, mood stabilizers or mood stabilizing antipsychotics). Atypical antipsychotics (eg, quetiapine, olanzapine), which antagonize dopamine receptors and modulate serotonin activity, possess mood stabilizing properties and demonstrate effectiveness for acute mania over the course of days. Alternatively, for acute bipolar depression, the most effective medication choices are lurasidone, quetiapine, lamotrigine, and fluoxetine-olanzapine. Once acute mood episodes resolve, patients with bipolar disorder should continue mood stabilizer maintenance therapy to prevent future episodes of mania or depression.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Diazepam (Choice A) may be utilized in conjunction with mood stabilizers or antipsychotic medications to promote sleep, which quickens the resolution of acute mania. However, acute mania requires treatment with a mood stabilizing medication. Fluoxetine (Choice B) and sertraline (Choice E) are selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors utilized for unipolar major depressive disorder. In patients with bipolar disorder, these medications may worsen mania or convert depression to mania.
