questions.stem: A 20-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his parents because he has not moved or spoken for 60 minutes despite encouragement. He has had similar episodes over the past 6 months. His parents say that he has always been shy and a loner. He has not looked for a job since graduating from high school 2 years ago. He appears unkempt and has long fingernails. He is mute and maintains a rigid posture during the examination. He resists passive movement of his extremities. Physical examination shows no other abnormalities. A CT scan of the head is normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Catatonia is a neurobehavioral syndrom1e marked by the inability to move or speak or by the inability to stop moving or speaking repetitively. It typically arises from an exacerbation of a psychiatric disorder such as schizophrenia. Patients with the stuporous subtype of catatonia typically present with immobility, mutism, muscle rigidity, waxy flexibility, negativism, staring, or posturing. Lab Values Calculator

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Catatonia is a neurobehavioral syndrome marked by the inability to move or speak (stuporous subtype) or the inability to stop moving or speaking repetitively (excited subtype) that typically arises from an exacerbation of a psychiatric disorder such as bipolar disorder or schizophrenia. Patients with the stuporous subtype of catatonia typically present with immobility, mutism, muscle rigidity, waxy flexibility (maintaining limb position after the limb is moved by the examiner), negativism (resistance to instruction), staring, and/or posturing. Patients with the excited subtype typically demonstrate hyperactivity, purposeless movements, verbigeration (repetition of words/phrases), and echolalia (repetition of other people's words). Treatment requires benzodiazepine medications or, if refractory to benzodiazepines, electroconvulsive therapy. According to the DSM-5, patients with schizophrenia spectrum disorders (eg, schizophrenia, schizoaffective disorder) demonstrate at least two of the following five symptoms: delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, or negative symptoms (eg, flat affect, apathy, alogia). To meet the diagnostic criteria for schizophrenia, patients must demonstrate functional deficits, and the total duration of symptoms must exceed 6 months. This patient demonstrates apathy (not looking for a job and poor hygiene) and consequent impairment in social function and can be assumed to possess a chronic psychotic disorder.

questions.stem: A previously well-functioning 14-year-old boy is apprehended for stealing a compact disc from a store. A court-ordered evaluation shows the stealing to be the latest in a series of self-defeating behaviors over the past 2 months that include incomplete homework assignments and truancy. He states that he has difficulty concentrating and awakens frequentlly at night. During an interview with his family, the teenager seems oblivious to the obvious marital discord between his parents. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Adjustment disorder refers to the onset of disproportionate distress within 3 months of an identifiable stressor and may be associated with anxiety, depression, and/or conduct disturbances. Conduct disturbances frequently manifest as acting out (eg, truant behavior). Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Adjustment disorder refers to the onset of an emotional disturbance within 3 months of an identifiable stressor. The emotional disturbance manifests as marked distress out of proportion to the stressor and a significant impairment in daily school, work, or social functioning. Patients may internalize or externalize their distress, resulting in depression or anxiety symptoms or conduct disturbances (acting out), respectively. Patients with adjustment disorder do not meet criteria of anxiety, depressive, or conduct disorders. This patient has some anxiety/depression symptoms (eg, difficulty concentrating and sleeping) and conduct disturbances (eg, truancy) with a clear stressor and without meeting the criteria for anxiety, depressive, or conduct disorders. As adjustment disorder is typically self- limited, the treatment of adjustment disorder commonly includes brief supportive psychotherapy.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Antisocial personality disorder (Choice B) is a cluster B personality disorder that arises from a deficit in empathy, resulting in pervasive violations of others' rights, aggression, and a hostile and manipulative attitude toward others. Personality disorders are only diagnosed in patients older than 18 years, and this patient does not yet demonstrate a persistent pattern of transgressive behavior. Conduct disorder (Choice C) refers to a persistent pattern of violating others' rights, including aggression, deceitful behaviors, and property destruction. Patients must demonstrate this behavior for 12 months, whereas this patient has been acting out for the past 2 months. questions.stem: A 42-year-old man with obsessive-compulsive disorder comes to the physician because of only partial symptomatic relief with adequate trials of clomipramine and fluoxetine. He has fewer but persistent contamination obsessions and conducts fewer hand-washing rituals. He asks what else can be done to relieve his symptoms. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
questions.educationalObjective: First-line treatment of OCD includes high doses of SSRls or CBT with a focus on exposure to the discomfort of suppressing the compulsion after experiencing the obsession. In patients with a partial response to SSRI monotherapy, physicians may augment the SSRI with CBT. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Obsessive-compulsive disorderr (OCD) is an anxiety disorder typically characterized by obsessions (unwanted, intrusive thoughts that produce anxiety) that the patient attempts to neutralize with compulsions (repetitive behaviors). By definition, the compulsions are excessive or do not realistically address the obsessive fears, and patients commonly recognize the irrationality of their compulsions. Treatment includes high doses of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRls) or cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT), also referred to as behavior therapy, with a focus on exposure to the discomfort of suppressing the compulsion after experiencing the obsession. Suppressing the compulsion teaches the patient that they can cope with the anxiety related to their obsessions and leads to natural habituation (decrease) of their anxiety. In patients who opt to try medication first and experience a partial response, physicians may augment with an antipsychotic medication (eg, risperidone) or CBT.

Incorrect Answers: A, B, D, E, F, G, and H. Seclusion and restraint (Choice A) is utilized on patients with acute physical agitation that presents an imminent danger to self or others. This patient is not an imminent danger to himself or others. SPECT scan of the brain (Choice B) may assist in diagnosing dementia, Alzheimer type, and other neurodegenerative diseases but is not typically utilized to confirm OCD diagnoses. Hypnotic regression therapy (Choice D) and insight-oriented psychotherapy (Choice E) attempt to uncover unconscious drivers of psychiatric disease. CBT has the most evidence for the treatment of OCD, as it directly targets problematic compulsive behaviors. Clozapine therapy (Choice G) is an atypical antipsychotic medication utilized in treatment-refractory schizophrenia. Serious side effects such as agranulocytosis, myocarditis, and seizures prohibit the use of clozapine as an augmentation agent for OCD. Electroconvulsive therapy (Choice H) is not commonly utilized in OCD, though a few case reports note effectiveness.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman comes to the physician because of difficulty falling asleep for 3 years. She reports that it has been even more difficult to fall asleep during the past month. Five weeks ago, her cat of 4 years was hit by a car and died. She has a generally poor appetite but has not had any recent changes in weight or appetite. She has worked as a registered nurse on the overnight shift at a skilled nursing care facility for 5 years. She says that she has never missed a day of work in 3 years, although she rarely feels like getting out of bed. She states that she prefers night work because it involves less contact with families. She does not enjoy her job even though she is respected for her work and regularly receives good evaluations. She is neatly dressed. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 70/min, respirations are 10/min, and blood pressure is 120/65 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no other abnormalities. On mental status examination, she is pleasant and polite. She says she felt sad when her cat died but states that she cannot recall a time when her life did not seem sad to her. When asked about her social life, she says she has never been in a romantic relationship and states that "no one would really want me." Results of laboratory studies are within the reference range. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Reassurance that this is normal bereavement

answerChoices.label: F
answerChoices.text: Supplementation with a multivitamin and iron

questions.educationalObjective: Persistent depressive disorder refers to at least three depression symptoms (including depressed mood during more days than not) that have endured for at least 2 years. Other symptoms of persistent depressive disorder include neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbances, appetite disturbances), impaired concentration, low self-esteem, and hopelessness. Similar to major depressive disorder, management should include both antidepressant medication and psychotherapy. Lab Values Calculator

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Persistent depressive disorder, previously called dysthymia, refers to at least three depression symptoms that have endured for at least 2 years. By definition, patients must endorse a depressed mood on more days than not. Other symptoms of persistent depressive disorder include neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbances, appetite disturbances), impaired concentration, low self-esteem, and hopelessness. This patient also demonstrates anhedonia as evidenced by a lack of enjoyment of her job or socializing; however, she does not have at least five symptoms of major depressive disorder, making persistent depressive disorder the most accurate diagnosis. Emerging evidence indicates that persistent depressive disorder should be managed similarly to major depressive disorder: antidepressant medication plus psychotherapy is most effective. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors are generally the first-line medications given their low side-effect burden. However, bupropion is also a reasonable option, and its activating effects may be useful in this patient with difficulty getting out of bed.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Reassurance that this is normal bereavement (Choice A) would be inappropriate. This patient's depression symptoms preceded the Lab Values Calculator Persistent depressive disorder, previously called dysthymia, refers to at least three depression symptoms that have endured for at least 2 years. By definition, patients must endorse a depressed mood on more days than not. Other symptoms of persistent depressive disorder include neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbances, appetite disturbances), impaired concentration, low self-esteem, and hopelessness. This patient also demonstrates anhedonia as evidenced by a lack of enjoyment of her job or socializing; however, she does not have at least five symptoms of major depressive disorder, making persistent depressive disorder the most accurate diagnosis. Emerging evidence indicates that persistent depressive disorder should be managed similarly to major depressive disorder: antidepressant medication plus psychotherapy is most effective. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors are generally the first-line medications given their low side-effect burden. However, bupropion is also a reasonable option, and its activating effects may be useful in this patient with difficulty getting out of bed. Incorrect Answers: A, C, D, E, F, and G. Reassurance that this is normal bereavement (Choice A) would be inappropriate. This patient's depression symptoms preceded the loss of her cat and meet the diagnostic criteria of persistent depressive disorder. Buspirone therapy (Choice C) is a serotonin modulator utilized to treat generalized anxiety disorder. It has no proven efficacy in persistent depressive disorder. Levothyroxine therapy (Choice D) is appropriate in patients with hypothyroidism. However, in addition to depression symptoms, patients with hypothyroidism typically demonstrate other physical examination findings (eg, dry skin, pretibial myxedema, coarse hair). Modafinil therapy (Choice E) is commonly utilized to address daytime sleepiness in patients with narcolepsy, obstructive sleep apnea, and shift-work sleep disorder. Modafinil would not address this patient's other depression symptoms. Supplementation with a multivitamin and iron (Choice F) assists in improving mood disorders related to nutritional deficiencies (eg, vitamin B 12, iron deficiency). However, low self-esteem is more consistent with persistent depressive disorder, and nutritional deficiencies are typically associated with other physical examination findings (eg, subacute combined degeneration in vitamin B 12 deficiency). Zaleplon therapy (Choice G) is commonly utilized in patients with acute insomnia (eg, secondary to adjustment disorder). However, pharmacotherapy is not typically as effective as psychotherapy for chronic insomnia. Further, zaleplon would not address this patient's other depression symptoms.

questions.stem: A 17-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her parents for a physical examination prior to participating in school sports. Her parents are concerned about her academic performance because she has received mostly B's and C's. Her parents and older brother attended Ivy League colleges. Every time they try to discuss applying to colleges, she becomes angry and tearful and goes to her room, slamming the door and refusing to talk about it. They try to talk to her through the closed door, but she will not cooperate. They have arranged alumni interviews for her at their own alma maters, and they are concerned that this negative attitude will impact her interviews. When they contacted her guidance counselor about their concerns and observations, he was surprised and reported that she has been diligently working on her applications with his help. She is at the 50th percentile for height and 30th percentile for weight. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she is cooperative but reticent. Her speech is articulate and coherent. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for these findings?
questions.educationalObjective: Unrealistically high parental expectations commonly lead to the child feeling disappointed and inadequate. These feelings may be internalized (eg, lead to depression) or externalized (eg, acting out), which are age-appropriate responses. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Parental expectations exert a strong influence on children's well-being. Unrealistically high expectations commonly lead to the child feeling disappointed and inadequate. These feelings may be internalized (eg, lead to depression) or externalized (eg, acting out, such as slamming the door). This patient's emotional dysregulation and uncooperative behavior when her parents communicate or imply high expectations about her academic performance is an age-appropriate reaction. Parents should critically examine whether their expectations are realistic and communicate acceptance to their child to avoid these emotional difficulties.

A 72-year-old man who is a resident of a skilled nursing care facility is brought to the emergency department because of sweating and increasing confusion for 2 days. He underwent a below-the-knee amputation 4 weeks ago for osteomyelitis. He has a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus treated with glyburide. He also is receiving acetaminophen and codeine for pain, allopurinol for gout, and haloperidol for nighttime agitation. His temperature is 39.5°C (103.1 °F), pulse is 120/min, and blood pressure is 130/90 mm Hg. Muscle tone is increased. The patient is too disoriented to respond appropriately to questions. questions.educationalObjective: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome is a life-threatening condition associated with the antagonism of dopamine that presents with altered mental status, muscle rigidity, hyperthermia, and autonomic dysfunction. Characteristic laboratory abnormalities include leukocytosis and electrolyte abnormalities along with increased creatine kinase, alkaline phosphatase, and lactate dehydrogenase. Lab Values Calculator

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) is the most likely diagnosis in this patient who has received high-potency antipsychotic medication (haloperidol). NMS is a life-threatening condition associated with the antagonism of dopamine that presents with altered mental status, muscle rigidity, hyperthermia, and autonomic dysfunction (eg, tachycardia, sweating). Common causative agents are antipsychotics (especially high-potency antipsychotics) and antidopaminergic nausea medications. Rigidity results from dopamine antagonism in the nigrostriatal pathway, while autonomic dysfunction and hyperthermia may result from dopamine antagonism in the hypothalamus. Characteristic laboratory abnormalities include leukocytosis and increased serum creatine kinase, lactate dehydrogenase, and alkaline phosphatase concentrations (caused by prolonged muscle rigidity and consequent myocyte damage). Electrolyte abnormalities (eg, hypo- or hypernatremia, hypocalcemia) may also be demonstrated. Treatment is supportive and includes discontinuation of the offending medication, hydration, cooling blankets, and benzodiazepines, if needed, for agitation.

Exertional heat stroke (Choice C) refers to intense exercise leading to multisystem effects, commonly presenting with confusion, acute kidney injury, liver injury, and rhabdomyolysis (which may present with muscle rigidity). A temperature greater than 40°C (104°F) is a diagnostic criterion of heat stroke. Further, this patient recovering in a skilled nursing facility from a below-the-knee amputation is unlikely able to exercise intensely enough to cause a heat stroke. Poor glucose control (Choice D) could lead to a hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state or diabetic ketoacidosis, which may present with confusion, lethargy, polyuria, and polydipsia. However, diaphoresis and muscle rigidity would be atypical, and this patient's blood glucose concentration is only mildly increased. Septicemia (Choice E) refers to a bloodstream infection and is characterized by fever, hypotension, tachycardia, and leukocytosis. In severe cases, altered mentation may occur along with signs of organ dysfunction (eg, oliguria). This patient does not demonstrate hypotension or leukocytosis consistent with sepsis, and muscle rigidity is atypical of sepsis.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old woman comes to the physician to request a sedative to help calm her nerves. She has had extreme anxiety and difficulty eating and sleeping since she received the diagnosis of breast cancer 10 days ago. She is married and has two children, and she worries constantly about what will happen to them if she dies. She regularly volunteers at the school library, but she has been unable to go because of her anxiety since her diagnosis. She has not felt depressed, guilty, or suicidal. She has no other history of serious medical or psychiatric illness. There is no family history of psychiatric illness. Physical examination shows a healing incision over the breast biopsy site. On mental status examination, she is anxious but shows a full range of affect. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Adjustment disorder refers to the onset of disproportionate distress within 3 months of an identifiable stressor and may be associated with anxiety, depression, or conduct disturbances without meeting the criteria for depressive or anxiety disorders. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Adjustment disorder refers to the onset of an emotional disturbance within 3 months of an identifiable stressor. The emotional disturbance manifests as marked distress out of proportion to the stressor and a significant impairment in daily school, work, or social functioning. Patients may internalize or externalize their distress, resulting in depression and anxiety symptoms or conduct disturbances, respectively. Patients with adjustment disorder do not meet criteria of anxiety, depressive, or conduct disorders. This patient has some anxiety and depression symptoms (anxiety, insomnia, decreased appetite) with a clear stressor and without meeting the criteria for anxiety, depressive, or conduct disorders. As adjustment disorder is typically self-limited, the treatment of adjustment disorder commonly includes brief supportive psychotherapy.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Acute stress disorder (Choice A) refers to symptoms of intrusive thoughts or memories, changes in arousal and reactivity (eg, hypervigilance ), avoidance of reminders of trauma, and negative mood/thoughts that occur within 1 month of a traumatic event such as physical or sexual violence. This patient has not experienced a violent event and is not reporting intrusive thoughts, avoidance of reminders of her stressor (she is instead constantly worrying about the stressor), or a depressed mood. Generalized anxiety disorder (Choice C) is an anxiety disorder that features excessive worrying about a range of topics with associated functional impairment. This patient's anxiety is specific to her new diagnosis. Lab Values Calculator Correct Answer: B. questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman is brought to the physician by her family 1 week after the sudden onset of bizarre behavior that began after she had a disagreement with her supervisor at work. The supervisor told her family that she became confused and began to accuse him of monitoring her phone calls and watching her every move because he wanted to fire her. Her work performance had been fine. She told her husband that she heard the voices of her coworkers talking about her when she was driving home from work 2 days ago. She used cocaine briefly 5 years ago. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she is oriented to person and place but not to date. She is unsure why she is being evaluated, and her answers to questions are incomprehensible at times. She admits to being worried and depressed about her coworkers being "after her." Laboratory studies are within normal limits, and toxicology screening shows traces of marijuana. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

answerChoices.label: A

questions.educationalObjective: Brief psychotic disorder is characterized by at least one acute psychotic symptom (eg, delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior) lasting less than 1 month. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient meets the diagnostic criteria for brief psychotic disorder. Brief psychotic disorder is characterized by the acute onset of one or more psychotic symptoms (eg, delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior) lasting less than 1 month. This patient is experiencing a paranoid delusion, disorganized speech, and auditory hallucinations. Risk factors for brief psychotic disorder include stressful life events and personality disorders. Treatment may include hospitalization and antipsychotic treatment depending on symptom severity and functional impairment, though some symptoms or the entire presentation may resolve without medications.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her parents because of behavior problems since starting preschool 3 months ago. Her parents report that she was adopted at 2 weeks of age and little is known about her biologic parents. Her teachers report that she is more active than her peers, is easily frustrated, and has frequent tantrums. She is not yet toilet trained. She is at the 20th percentile for height and weight. Physical examination shows a thin upper lip, a narrow philtrum, and bilateral palmar creases. Her gait is normal. On mental status examination, she speaks clearly, communicating in two- to three-word phrases. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Alcohol is a known teratogen that causes fetal alcohol syndrome when used during pregnancy. Fetal alcohol syndrome presents with intellectual disability and developmental delays, along with physical malformations such as microcephaly, facial dysmorphia, limb deformities, and congenital heart disease. Lab Values Calculator

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Alcohol is a proven teratogen, since alcohol use during pregnancy leads to irreversible fetal neurologic effects and physical malformations in a cluster of symptoms called fetal alcohol syndrome. Fetal alcohol syndrome is a leading cause of intellectual disability in the United States and would explain this patient's behavioral problems. Because of the failure of cell migration, fetal alcohol syndrome also presents with physical abnormalities such as microcephaly, facial abnormalities (small palpebral fissures, thin vermilion border, cleft lip and palate, smooth or long philtrum), limb deformities, and congenital heart disease. Patients with fetal alcohol syndrome may demonstrate abnormal bone development (eg, cervical spine fusion) and delayed bone growth. Though alcohol is associated with obstetric complications such as preterm birth that commonly result from the use of substances during pregnancy, the physical malformations including those seen in this case are specific to alcohol use.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) (Choice A) is defined as a pattern of inattention or hyperactivity that persists in at least two settings. Patients may demonstrate symptoms of inattention, such as difficulty following instructions, difficulty completing tasks, or a poor attention span in school. Interrupting others, fidgeting, and not waiting one's turn may be symptoms of hyperactivity. This patient's behavioral problems are only seen at school and her unique physical characteristics are not seen in ADHD. Characteristic physical findings of Down syndrome (Choice B) include intellectual disability, broad, flat, facial features with prominent epicanthal folds, and a single palmar crease. There are similarities between the abnormal facies of patients with fetal alcohol syndrome and Down syndrome, but this patient's combination of growth retardation and characteristic physical features are most suggestive of fetal alcohol syndrome. Fragile X syndrome (Choice D) is an X-linked dominant cause of inherited intellectual disability that presents as a result of a CGG- trinucleotide repeat expansion in the FMR1 gene. questions.stem: A 22-year-old college student comes to the physician because of depressed mood, restlessness, irritability, and an inability to concentrate since being raped 1 week ago. She has had difficulty sleeping because of recurrent nightmares about the rape and has not been able to study or attend classes. At times, she feels like the world around her is not real. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she is anxious and tense. She is unable to remember the details of the rape and becomes distressed and does not want to talk about it. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Acute stress disorder refers to symptoms of intrusive thoughts or memories, changes in arousal and reactivity, avoidance of reminders of trauma, and negative mood/thoughts that occur within 1 month of a traumatic event such as physical or sexual violence. explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Acute stress disorder refers to symptoms of intrusive thoughts or memories, changes in arousal and reactivity, avoidance of reminders of trauma, and negative changes in mood or thoughts that occur within 1 month of a traumatic event. Intrusive memories may manifest as daytime flashbacks, nightmares, or dissociative experiences (eg, derealization as in this patient). Changes in arousal and reactivity may manifest as hypervigilance, easy startle, anxiety, restlessness, or decreased concentration. This patient's selective amnesia represents a negative alteration in cognition. Treatment commonly includes trauma-focused cognitive behavioral therapy rather than medication.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with cancer of the tongue comes to the physician 2 days after an episode of anxiety and panic while being fitted with a mask in preparation for radiation therapy. He reports similar symptoms during MRI of the brain; he says he was unable to tolerate being confined to such a small space and felt panic and the need to escape. On physical examination, the tongue is enlarged and tender. On mental status examination, he is anxious when describing being confined. The most appropriate pharmacotherapy for this patient targets which of the following neurotransmitters?
questions.educationalObjective: Specific phobia is a psychiatric disorder featuring anxiety about a certain situation or object that leads to avoidance behavior. Treatment commonly includes cognitive behavioral therapy or benzodiazepine medications, which are positive allosteric modulators of y-aminobutyric acid type A (GABAA) receptors. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Specific phobia is a psychiatric disorder featuring anxiety about a certain situation or object that leads to avoidance behavior. Common phobias include flying, animals, and enclosed spaces. To meet diagnostic criteria, the anxiety symptoms must be persistent (lasting longer than 6 months), and patients must demonstrate significant distress or social and occupational impairment. Long-term cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) is the most effective treatment, though short-term benzodiazepines may be utilized in certain patients whose feared situations occur infrequently (or patients without access to CBT). Benzodiazepines are positive allosteric modulators of the ionotropic y-aminobutyric acid type A (GABAA) receptor, permitting the increased influx of chloride ions and decreased neuronal excitability in brain regions responsible for fear such as the amygdala.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Medications that increase acetylcholine (Choice A) signaling (eg, cholinesterase inhibitors such as donepezil) are utilized in dementia and myasthenia gravis. Acetylcholine antagonists (eg, benztropine) lead to relief of drug-induced parkinsonism along with sedation, urinary retention, and constipation. These medications are not utilized to treat acute anxiety. Dopamine (Choice C) agonists (eg, pramipexole) are utilized for symptomatic treatment in Parkinson disease, while dopamine antagonists (eg, antipsychotic medications) are utilized to treat schizophrenia and bipolar disorder. These medications are less effective than benzodiazepines in treating acute anxiety. Norepinephrine antagonists (Choice D) (eg, -adrenergic block,ers such as propranolol) are commonly utilized for performance anxiety but not specific phobia. Norepinephrine agonists (eg, stimulant medications, clonidine) are commonly used to treat attention- deficit/hyperactivity disorder. Norepinephrine antagonists (Choice D) (eg, -adrenergic block,ers such as propranolol) are commonly utilized for performance anxiety but not specific phobia. Norepinephrine agonists (eg, stimulant medications, clonidine) are commonly used to treat attention- deficit/hyperactivity disorder. Clonidine is also utilized off-label to treat anxiety, though benzodiazepines are the most effective option for acute anxiety. Serotonin (Choice E) agonists (eg, buspirone) and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (eg, sertraline) are utilized to treat anxiety and depressive disorders on a long-term basis, though these take weeks to work and would not be effective in treating acute anxiety. Combined serotonin receptor antagonists and reuptake inhibitors (eg, trazodone) are utilized to treat insomnia and depression.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old man comes to the physician because of decreased libido over the past 2 years. He describes his marriage as good. He is able to achieve erection only through masturbation. He does not smoke cigarettes or drink alcohol. He uses marijuana daily. His pulse is 72/min, respirations are 10/min, and blood pressure is 120/90 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Serum studies show a thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration of 1 µU/ml, thyroxine (T4) concentration of 7 µg/dl, and triiodothyronine (T3) concentration of 125 ng/dl. Urine toxicology screening is positive for marijuana. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
questions.educationalObjective: Cannabis use may lead to decreased libido and erectile dysfunction. The initial diagnostic and therapeutic step for patients with evidence of marijuana-associated erectile dysfunction is to discontinue cannabis use. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Cannabis use has been associated with erectile dysfunction. The pathogenesis is believed to involve both peripheral vascular endothelial dysfunction and dysregulated endocannabinoid signaling in the hypothalamus. Testicular atrophy and decreased libido related to cannabis use have been found in animal studies, though human studies of the relationship of cannabis and libido have demonstrated mixed results. Erectile function may be sufficient to allow for nocturnal erections and erections from direct penile stimulation but insufficient to permit the level of rigidity required for penetration. Management of erectile dysfunction requires addressing the underlying cause. To assist in determining the underlying cause and potentially addressing the problem, patients with erectile dysfunction who use cannabis should be advised to discontinue cannabis use.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Individual psychotherapy (Choice B) and marital therapy (Choice C) assist in addressing psychogenic causes of erectile dysfunction, which typically present with intact nocturnal and masturbation-induced erections. Marital therapy is unlikely to be helpful given the good relationship between the patient and his partner. This patient should first attempt discontinuing cannabis; if discontinuing cannabis does not lead to improved sexual function, then individual psychotherapy should be considered to address potential performance anxiety or another psychological cause. Levothyroxine therapy (Choice D) treats sexual dysfunction secondary to hypothyroidism. This patient demonstrates normal thyroid function tests. Sildenafil therapy (Choice E) promotes vasodilation in the arteries of the corpora cavernosa. In patients with erectile dysfunction secondary to psychological causes or substance use, these underlying problems should be addressed first. Lab Values Calculator '. questions.stem: An 18-year-old woman comes to the physician because she is anxious and concerned that she is contaminating her hands with HIV whenever she touches a door handle. She realizes that this is irrational but nevertheless has to wash her hands repeatedly with household bleach. She estimates that she washes her hands 10 times daily for 10 minutes at a time. Examination shows cracked and bleeding hands. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?
questions.educationalObjective: OCD is an anxiety disorder typically characterized by obsessions (unwanted, intrusive thoughts that produce anxiety) that the patient attempts to neutralize with compulsions (repetitive behaviors). OCD is most commonly treated with CBT or SSRI medications. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Obsessive-compulsive disorderr (OCD) is an anxiety disorder typically characterized by obsessions (unwanted, intrusive thoughts that produce anxiety) that the patient attempts to neutralize with compulsions (repetitive behaviors). By definition, the compulsions are excessive or do not realistically address the obsessive fears, and patients commonly recognize the irrationality of their compulsions. This patient's worry about contamination represents an obsession, while her repeated hand washing represents a compulsion. Treatment includes high doses of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRls, eg, fluoxetine) or cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) with a focus on exposure to the discomfort of suppressing the compulsion after experiencing the obsession. SSRls have the lowest side effect burden of antidepressant medications. Clomipramine (a tricyclic antidepressant) and venlafaxine (a selective serotonin- norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor antidepressant) are effective second-line agents.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Bupropion (Choice A) is an antidepressant that increases synaptic norepinephrine and dopamine. It commonly causes increased anxiety as a side effect and is not a first-line medication for OCD. Buspirone (Choice B) is a serotonin modulator utilized to treat generalized anxiety disorder. It has no proven efficacy in OCD. Haloperidol (Choice D) is a typical antipsychotic medication utilized to treat schizophrenia. It has no proven efficacy in OCD. lmipramine (Choice E) is a tricyclic antidepressant without proven efficacy for OCD and an increased side effect burden compared with Lab Values Calculator G) Propranolol Correct Answer: C. questions.stem: Immediately after undergoing operative repair of a right knee dislocation, a 42-year-old woman has severe pain. She has a history of heroin addiction and has been receiving therapy through a community-based methadone maintenance program. Her methadone maintenance dose has remained constant for 2 years. She has no other history of medical or psychiatric illness. On examination, she rates her pain as a 10 on a 10-point scale, a level that is consistent with that reported by other patients who have undergone this operative procedure. Administration of which of the following is most appropriate at this time?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: A decreased methadone dose and additional analgesia on a set schedule

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: A decreased methadone dose and patient-controlled analgesia

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: An increased methadone dose and additional analgesia on a set schedule

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: The patient's usual methadone maintenance dose and patient-controlled analgesia

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: The patient's usual methadone maintenance dose only

questions.educationalObjective: Optimal acute pain control for patients with substance use disorder on chronic methadone maintenance therapy includes continuing their baseline methadone dose and adding additional non-opioid and opioid medications on a flexible schedule, such as patient-controlled analgesia. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Methadone is an opioid receptor agonist with a long half-life. While it can be used to treat acute pain, it is often used to treat patients with opioid use disorder. While methadone is commonly dosed once daily when used to treat opioid use disorder, it only provides analgesia for 6 to 8 hours. Therefore, it is not solely efficient for the management of postoperative pain. Current recommendations for acute pain management in patients on methadone maintenance therapy include continuing the baseline methadone dose and supplementing pain control with other non-opioid and opioid agents as needed. Pain control may be more difficult in patients on chronic methadone therapy, as they commonly have cross-tolerance to other opioids. Therefore, continuing this patient's baseline methadone dose and adding a regimen of patient-controlled analgesia (PCA) would be appropriate to allow for sufficient pain relief. As her acute pain declines, the PCA can be weaned.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A decreased methadone dose and additional analgesia on a set schedule (Choice A) would not effectively treat this patient's pain. A decreased dose of methadone would likely exacerbate her pain, and a set schedule of pain medication would not be adequate or flexible enough to control her pain. A decreased methadone dose and patient-controlled analgesia (Choice B) may control this patient's pain but would likely cause her to use an increased amount of intravenous opioids as compared with a regimen with her baseline dose of methadone. An increased methadone dose and additional analgesia on a set schedule (Choice C) may control this patient's pain but would complicate weaning of her medication regimen back to her baseline methadone dose. Additionally, this patient has been on a stable dose of methadone and adjusting her dose may interfere with her substance use disorder treatment. questions.stem: A 15-year-old boy is brought to the physician by his parents because of a 3-month history of social withdrawal and subtle but increasingly odd interpersonal interactions, such as blinking to avoid direct eye contact. He also has a 1-month history of intermittent daydreaming and episodes of talking to himself. His behavior was first noticed by his teacher and the school nurse, who notified his parents. The patient says that when in quiet locations, he hears vague, distorted sounds. He talks to himself to calm himself and to make the sounds disappear. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. His maternal uncle has bipolar disorder, and two paternal uncles have schizophrenia. The patient does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he is alert and fully oriented; he appears shy and has a flat affect. He says his mood is "okay." Further testing is most likely to show which of the following results in this patient?
questions.educationalObjective: Patients with schizophrenia commonly demonstrate early cognitive dysfunction across several domains: attention, working memory, verbal learning and memory, and executive function. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: According to the DSM-5, patients with schizophreniform disorder must demonstrate two of following: delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, or disorganized behavior (known as positive symptoms), or negative symptoms (eg, flat affect, apathy, alogia). To meet diagnostic criteria, patients must demonstrate symptoms for greater than 1 month and less than 6 months. Two-thirds of patients with schizophreniform disorder ultimately develop schizophrenia. Cognitive impairment is commonly one of the early signs of schizophrenia. Schizophrenia has been postulated to result from excessive pruning of glutamatergic synapses in the cerebral cortex during development, explaining the negative and cognitive symptoms of schizophrenia. Patients commonly experience deficits across several cognitive domains including attention, working memory, verbal learning and memory, and executive function. However, basic cognitive functions such as orientation commonly remain intact. Antipsychotic medications address positive symptoms but do not significantly improve negative or cognitive symptoms. Cognitive training may assist with cognitive deficits.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Abnormal amyloid precursor protein (Choice A) and hyperphosphorylation of the Tau protein (Choice E) are pathologic findings in dementia, Alzheimer type, not schizophrenia. Amyloid plaques are extracellular protein accumulations while tau forms intracytoplasmic and extracellular protein tangles. Adolescent patients are too young to develop dementia, Alzheimer type. Decreased auditory acuity (Choice C) and decreased visual fields (Choice D) are atypical of schizophrenia. Patients with schizophrenia typically experience deficits in higher-order functions mediated by the frontal lobes such as cognition and motivation (eg, cognitive and negative symptoms). questions.stem: Three weeks after voluntarily entering a residential treatment facility for alcoholism, a 27-year-old woman is being prepared for discharge. She requests medication to control her urge to drink alcohol and decrease her risk for relapse. Prior to admission, she drank 6 to 12 glasses of wine nightly. She tried to quit drinking several times but says her urges to drink returned after 2 to 3 days of abstinence. She has no other history of serious illness and takes no medications. Physical and mental status examinations show no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?
questions.educationalObjective: Alcohol use disorder is characterized by an inability to cut back on drinking, excessive time spent drinking, and negative consequences related to drinking. Patients with moderate to severe alcohol use disorder may benefit from naltrexone, an opioid receptor antagonist that prevents alcohol cravings. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Alcohol use disorder refers to an addiction to alcohol that must meet at least two criteria, including an inability to cut back on drinking, drinking more than expected, excessive time spent drinking, alcohol cravings, persistent drinking despite physical or mental problems related to drinking, and persistent drinking despite negative social consequences. Patients with moderate to severe alcohol use disorder may benefit from daily medication. Commonly utilized medications for alcohol use disorder include naltrexone, acamprosate, disulfiram, and gabapentin. Naltrexone, a long-acting opioid receptor antagonist, blocks the rewarding effects of drinking alcohol and thinking about alcohol, thereby preventing alcohol cravings.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Buprenorphine (Choice A) is an opioid receptor partial agonist utilized as an opioid replacement therapy in opioid use disorder. It is not useful in alcohol use disorder. Bupropion (Choice B) is an antidepressant medication that increases synaptic dopamine and norepinephrine and is postulated to antagonize nicotine receptors. It is useful in nicotine use disorder but not alcohol use disorder. As a result of its lowering of the seizure threshold, bupropion increases the risk for alcohol withdrawal seizures. Levomethadyl acetate (Choice C) is an opioid receptor agonist similar to methadone. It is utilized as an opioid replacement therapy in refractory opioid use disorder but would not be helpful in alcohol use disorder. Naloxone (Choice D) is a short-acting opioid receptor antagonist. Its maximum duration of action is approximately 2 hours, which would necessitate multiple doses daily to prevent alcohol cravings. It is generally not used for this purpose; rather, it is a commonly employed Lab Values Calculator B) C) D) E) Bupropion Levomethadyl acetate Naloxone Naltrexone Correct Answer: E. It is generally not used for this purpose; rather, it is a commonly employed antidote for opiate overdose.

questions.stem: As a condition of employment, a newly hired police officer has agreed to avoid the use of all illicit drugs both on and off duty and to provide random urine samples for drug screening. Although he has not used any drugs in the past month, his urine sample shows a definite positive reaction. Which of the following drugs would most likely account for this finding?
questions.educationalObjective: Compared with other recreational drugs, cannabis has a long half-life. In chronic users, urine toxicology screenings may remain positive up to 50 days after the patient's last use. Lab Values Calculator

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: The psychoactive component of cannabis is f1-9 tetrahydrocannabinol (THC). THC is lipophilic and therefore distributes widely across body tissues (eg, adipose tissue, heart, lungs, and liver). As a result of its wide distribution and slow release from body stores, THC has a long half-life and may lead to positive urine toxicology screening up to 50 days after the last use. Patients who regularly use cannabis are more likely to demonstrate persistently positive urine drug screenings.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Cocaine (Choice A), heroin (Choice B), LSD (Choice C), and methamphetamine (Choice E) are not as widely distributed as cannabis and possess shorter half-lives. Urine drug screens are typically negative a few days after the patient's last use.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old man is brought to the physician by his parents because of changes in his behavior during the past 6 months. During this time, he has been obsessed with what he calls "the ultimate truth of the universe" and has spent most of his time in his room alone. His parents say that he has always been serious, curious, and quiet. The few friends he had stopped interacting with him because of this behavior. The patient explains that he needs to solve "the ultimate and consummate algorithmic result." He has no history of serious illness. He does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs. He is 173 cm (5 ft 8 in) tall and weighs 98 kg (217 lb); BMI is 33 kg/m2. His pulse is 82/min, and blood pressure is 116/77 mm Hg. Physical examination shows abdominal striae. On mental status examination, he describes his mood as "transfixed." He has a blunt affect. His thought process is disorganized, and he continues to talk about the equation. There is no evidence of suicidal ideation. Results of laboratory studies are within the reference ranges. Urine toxicology screening is negative. A CT scan of the head shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?
questions.educationalObjective: Patients with schizophrenia demonstrate six or more months of psychotic symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, or negative symptoms) associated with functional impairment. Treatment requires antipsychotic medication. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: According to the DSM-5, patients with schizophrenia spectrum disorders demonstrate at least two of the following five symptoms: delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, or negative symptoms (eg, flat or blunted affect, apathy, alogia). Delusions, hallucinations, and disorganization are referred to as positive symptoms. To meet the diagnostic criteria for schizophrenia, patients must demonstrate functional deficits, and the total duration of symptoms must exceed 6 months. This patient demonstrates delusional thinking (a fixed belief that he needs to solve a problem of the universe), disorganized thinking orspeech, and negative symptoms (isolating to his room, a blunted affect). Many patients with schizophrenia are socially anxious prior to the onset of psychotic symptoms. Management requires antipsychotic medication (eg, ziprasidone, an atypical antipsychotic). Antipsychotic medications antagonize the dopamine receptor, addressing excessive dopaminergic signaling in the mesolimbic pathway (postulated to underlie positive psychotic symptoms), though they do not address the negative or cognitive symptoms of schizophrenia.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Buspirone (Choice A) is a serotonin modulator utilized to treat generalized anxiety disorder. Clonazepam (Choice B) is a benzodiazepine medication utilized to treat acute episodes of anxiety. Neither would address psychotic symptoms. Lab Values Calculator E) Lithium carbonate F) Ziprasidone Correct Answer: F. According to the DSM-5, patients with schizophrenia spectrum disorders demonstrate at least two of the following five symptoms: delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, or negative symptoms (eg, flat or blunted affect, apathy, alogia). Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, D, and E. Buspirone (Choice A) is a serotonin modulator utilized to treat generalized anxiety disorder. Clonazepam (Choice B) is a benzodiazepine medication utilized to treat acute episodes of anxiety. Neither would address psychotic symptoms. Clozapine (Choice C) is an atypical antipsychotic medication utilized in treatment-refractory schizophrenia. Serious side effects such as agranulocytosis, myocarditis, and seizures prohibit the use of clozapine as an initial treatment. Fluoxetine (Choice D) is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor utilized to treat anxiety and depressive disorders. It does not target isolated psychotic symptoms in the absence of mood symptoms. This patient does not demonstrate significant mood changes, neurovegetative symptoms, or anxiety consistent with a depressive or anxiety disorder. Lithium carbonate (Choice E) is a mood stabilizer medication utilized in bipolar disorder. It is ineffective in treating isolated psychotic symptoms in the absence of mood symptoms. This patient does not demonstrate mood changes or changes in neurovegetative symptoms consistent with mania or depression.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old man comes to the physician for a routine health maintenance examination for insurance purposes. When asked for an emergency contact, he gives the name of his mother but says that she lives in another state and that he has not seen her for 5 years. He lives by himself and says that he has no friends. He spends most of his free time alone in his apartment cutting out articles about animals from magazines and newspapers that he plans to use in the future. He has worked the graveyard shift of his company for 10 years with few absences. On two occasions, he was offered a day shift but refused. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs. He is wearing mismatched socks, and his glasses are tied to a piece of twine he wears around his neck. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he is alert and oriented to person, place, and time. His thoughts are goal directed but difficult to understand because of his frequent use of idiosyncratic metaphors. He reports no auditory or visual hallucinations. He can recall three of three objects after 5 minutes and describes events of the past several years without difficulty. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Schizotypal personality disorder manifests as an enduring pattern of strange or eccentric beliefs and behavior, social detachment or suspiciousness, and abnormal perceptual experiences such as illusions. Personality disorders are persistently maladaptive ways of relating to the self and to society that typically appear by early adulthood. Lab Values Calculator

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Schizotypal personality disorder is a cluster A personality disorder, the odd or eccentric cluster. The disorder is characterized by odd behavior and thinking and a constricted affect. Patients with schizotypal personality disorder may have strange, overly metaphorical or magical thinking, though they fluctuate in the degree of their conviction about these beliefs (which differentiates these beliefs from the fixed delusions of schizophrenia). The strange beliefs may also manifest as eccentric appearance as in this patient. Patients may experience somatosensory illusions or other abnormal perceptions. Patients with schizotypal personality disorder may appear to lack interest in cultivating relationships (eg, work graveyard shifts) and, because of an inability to interpret others' motivations, may be deeply distrustful and anxious around others. Personality disorders are persistently maladaptive ways of relating to the self and to society that typically appear by early adulthood.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Agoraphobia (Choice A) refers to the fear of leaving one's home. Though this patient lacks an interest in socializing, he leaves his home to work the graveyard shift. Avoidant personality disorder (Choice C) is a cluster C personality disorder, the anxious or fearful cluster, that features the avoidance of interpersonal contact because of feelings of social inadequacy and hypersensitivity to rejection. This patient instead demonstrates social apathy. Obsessive-compulsive disorderr (Choice D) is an anxiety disorder typically characterized by obsessions (unwanted, intrusive thoughts that produce anxiety) that the patient attempts to neutralize with compulsions (repetitive behaviors). Though the patient repetitively cuts out animal pictures, this behavior appears to be motivated by strange or magical thinking rather than intrusive thoughts. Schizophrenia (Choice E) includes delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, or negative symptoms (eg, flat affect, apathy, alogia) with a total symptom duration of at least six months. Patients with schizophrenia typically demonstrate complex, rigid delusions, whereas patients with schizotypal personality disorder commonly demonstrate fluctuations in the tenacity with which they hold their strange beliefs. Magical thinking, metaphorical language, and an eccentric appearance without overt delusions or hallucinations are classic for schizotypal disorder. Social phobia (Choice G), or social anxiety disorder, refers to an excessive fear of scrutiny, embarrassment, or rejection in social or performance situations, leading to significant distress and/or impaired functioning. This patient instead demonstrates social apathy.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 2-week history of anxiety and difficulty falling asleep. He attributes his symptoms to the stress related to his 21-year-old daughter's wedding plans. He divorced his wife 11 years ago because he felt more sexually attracted to men than women. He has had a monogamous homosexual relationship for 7 years. He relates that he is happier now than when he was married, but he has been having some doubts recently. He would like to bring his partner to the wedding and accompany his daughter down the aisle, but the groom's parents are protesting his attendance. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Adjustment disorder refers to the onset of disproportionate distress within 3 months of an identifiable stressor and may be associated with anxiety, depression, and/or conduct disturbances. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Adjustment disorder refers to the onset of an emotional disturbance within 3 months of an identifiable stressor. The emotional disturbance manifests as marked distress out of proportion to the stressor and a significant impairment in daily school, work, or social functioning. Patients may internalize or externalize their distress, resulting in depression and anxiety symptoms or conduct disturbances, respectively. Patients with adjustment disorder do not meet criteria of anxiety, depressive, or conduct disorders. This patient is experiencing distress and anxiety symptoms related to the possibility of difficulties with his daughter's fiance's parents at his daughter's wedding because of his sexual orientation. As adjustment disorder is typically self-limited, the treatment of adjustment disorder commonly includes brief supportive psychotherapy. Incorrect Answer: B, C, D, and E. Exhibitionistic disorder (Choice B) is a paraphilia characterized by sexual arousal related to the exposure of the patient's genitalia that causes clinically significant distress or functional impairment. This patient does not demonstrate evidence of exhibitionistic disorder. Gender dysphoria (Choice C) refers to a difference between an individual's assigned gender and gender identity that causes clinically significant distress or functional impairment. Though this patient experiences sexual attraction to men, there is no evidence that he possesses a gender identity different from his assigned gender as a man. Generalized anxiety disorder (Choice D) is an anxiety disorder that features excessive worrying about a range of topics with associated functional impairment. This patient's anxiety is specific to his daughter's wedding. questions.stem: A 67-year-old woman with major depressive disorder is brought to the physician by her daughter for a follow-up examination. During the past 6 months, she has received trials of citalopram, fluvoxamine, and escitalopram with no improvement in her symptoms. Four weeks ago, she was switched from fluoxetine to sertraline therapy because the fluoxetine caused excessive tiredness. Her daughter reports that the patient is "slightly better, but not dramatically so." She has had a decreased appetite resulting in a 9-kg (20-lb) weight loss during the past 3 months. Today, her eyes are closed throughout most of the visit. She appears cachectic and disheveled. She is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 43 kg (95 lb); BMI is 16 kg/m2. Physical examination shows poor skin turgor and severe psychomotor retardation. On mental status examination, she responds to few of the physician's questions and uses only brief responses. There is no evidence of suicidal ideation or psychotic symptoms. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Add bupropion to her current medication regimen

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Add olanzapine to her current medication regimen

questions.educationalObjective: In patients with nutritional compromise secondary to depression, inpatient psychiatric hospitalization is necessary to maintain the patient's physical safety and initiate ECT. ECT involves the induction of a generalized seizure and is indicated in patients with refractory depression or depression associated with catatonia, nutritional compromise/dehydration, psychotic features, or persistent suicidal intent. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Symptoms of major depressive disorder (MDD) include two or more weeks of at least five of the following symptoms: depressed mood, anhedonia (eg, decreased interest in socializing), guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, suicidal thoughts, or neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance). These symptoms disrupt everyday functioning. This patient's functional impairment is so severe that she is minimally interactive and appears disheveled. Her neurovegetative symptoms have led to physical deterioration, including significant weight loss and nutritional compromise. The patient requires admission to the psychiatric unit to prevent further physical deterioration and so she can receive an inpatient course of electroconvulsive therapy (ECT). ECT utilizes electrical current to induce a generalized seizure under general anesthesia, which is postulated to increase monoamine activity and promote neurogenesis. Indications include depression that is refractory to multiple medication classes and depression associated with catatonia, nutritional compromise and dehydration, psychotic features, or persistent suicidal intent. Depression may begin to improve immediately after the first ECT session, whereas antidepressant medication takes weeks to work. Further, ECT has an increased effectiveness compared with antidepressant medications. Adverse events commonly include transient memory loss and the risks of general anesthesia (eg, cardiovascular events, postoperative confusion).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Adding bupropion or olanzapine to an antidepressant regimen (Choices A and B) are augmentation strategies utilized in patients with a significant partial response to the initial antidepressant. This patient minimally responded to sertraline. Further, these augmentation strategies typically take weeks to be effective, whereas this patient is nutritionally compromised and requires rapid treatment. questions.stem: A 25-year-old man is admitted to the hospital because of extreme hyperactivity for 2 days. He is unable to conduct an intelligent conversation because of flight of ideas. He is started on haloperidol therapy (10 mg daily). Two days later, he has difficulty breathing, uncontrollable tightening of his face and neck muscles with spasm, and a rolling up and locking of his eyes. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?
questions.educationalObjective: An acute dystonic reaction most commonly occurs in response to typical antipsychotics and presents with Lab Values Calculator A) B) C) D) E) Bethanechol Chlordiazepoxide Clonazepam Diphenhydramine Propranolol Correct Answer: D. Acute dystonic reactions are involuntary, painful muscle spasms that occur most frequently in response to potent dopamine-2 (D 2) antagonists such as typical antipsychotics (eg, haloperidol). Common presentations include torticollis (head tilted to the side and down), retrocollis (head tilted back), opisthotonos (arching of the back), and oculogyric crisis (upward deviation of the eyes). Patients rarely present with laryngospasm and strider. The underlying pathophysiology is an imbalance in the dopaminergic-cholinergic pathways of the basal ganglia. Risk factors for development include male sex, young age, recent cocaine use, and a history of previous acute dystonic reaction. Acute dystonic reactions are treated with diphenhydramine or benztropine, which are both anticholinergic medications that work to restore the proper balance in the dopaminergic and cholinergic pathways. Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, and E. Bethanechol (Choice A) is a muscarinic receptor agonist that promotes bladder and gastrointestinal motility because of its stimulation of smooth muscle. It does not act on skeletal muscle and would therefore be unhelpful in acute dystonic reactions. Chlordiazepoxide (Choice B) is a benzodiazepine medication most commonly utilized for alcohol withdrawal. Clonazepam (Choice C) is a benzodiazepine medication commonly utilized to treat anxiety and rapid-eye movement sleep behavior disorder. Benzodiazepines such as lorazepam may also be utilized to treat antipsychotic-induced akathisia. However, these medications do not restore the dopamine-acetylcholine balance in the basal ganglia and are therefore ineffective for an acute dystonic reaction. Propranolol (Choice E) is a -adrenergic blocker medication utilized to treat performance anxiety and antipsychotic-induced akathisia. However, propranolol does not restore the dopamine-acetylcholine balance in the basal ganglia and is therefore ineffective for an acute dystonic reaction. questions.stem: A 27-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by police after he threatened to commit suicide in jail. He was arrested 3 days ago for the murder of his girlfriend. Two years ago, he completed a 5-year prison sentence for assault. He was treated in a psychiatric hospital for conduct disorder at the age of 15 years. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He has a history of drinking excessive amounts of alcohol and using illicit drugs. He is cooperative with the hospital staff but becomes irritable when the police officer who is guarding him attempts to interact with him. His temperature is 36.6°C (97.8°F), pulse is 78/min, and blood pressure is 132/82 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities except for multiple scars over the face. On questioning, he says that he got the scars in fights. On mental status examination, there is no evidence of depression or psychosis. His speech is clear and slow, and he says, "If you don't release me and I am sent back to jail, I will find a way to kill myself!" Metabolites of which of the following neurotransmitters are most likely to be decreased in this patient's cerebrospinal fluid?
questions.educationalObjective: Antisocial personality disorder is a cluster B personality disorder, the emotional or dramatic cluster, that is postulated to arise from deficient serotonin signaling and consequent dysphoria. This dysphoric affect combined with a lack of empathy leads to pervasive violations of others' rights, aggression, and a hostile and manipulative attitude toward others. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Antisocial personality disorder is a cluster B personality disorder, the emotional or dramatic cluster, that arises from a deficit in empathy, resulting in pervasive violations of others' rights, aggression, and a hostile and manipulative attitude toward others. Similar to other cluster B personality disorders, patients with antisocial personality disorder struggle to regulate their emotions in that their frustration tolerance is low and they frequently behave impulsively. Deficient serotonin signaling secondary to genetic polymorphisms in the serotonin transporter has been found in patients with antisocial personality disorder and borderline personality disorder, another cluster B personality disorder characterized by impulsive self-harm behaviors. It is postulated that deficient serotonin leads to a propensity toward a dysphoric affect, which usually leads to self-harm behavior in women (reflecting borderline personality disorder) and violent behavior toward others in men (reflecting antisocial personality disorder). These patients may also be chronically bored and hence sensation seeking, leading to a high rate of substance abuse and gambling. These personality traits lead to deficits in personal (extreme egocentrism) and interpersonal (empathy, intimacy) functioning. Personality disorders are persistently maladaptive ways of relating to the self and to society that typically appear by early adulthood.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Acetylcholine (Choice A) is a neurotransmitter that mediates attention, learning, and memory. Acetylcholine signaling is deficient in dementia, Alzheimer type, but not strongly implicated in antisocial personality disorder. y-Aminobutyric acid (Choice B), GABA, is the primary inhibitory neurotransmitter. Acetylcholine signaling is deficient in dementia, Alzheimer type, but not strongly implicated in antisocial personality disorder. y-Aminobutyric acid (Choice B), GABA, is the primary inhibitory neurotransmitter. GABA deficiency is implicated in diseases of central nervous system hyperexcitability but not antisocial personality disorder. Dopamine (Choice C) is a monoamine neurotransmitter that mediates reward in the mesolimbic pathway and movement in the basal ganglia. Dopamine excess in the mesolimbic pathway is implicated in psychosis and may underlie antisocial personality disorder (signifying a hypersensitive reward pathway). Dopamine deficiency leads to parkinsonism but not antisocial personality disorder. Norepinephrine (Choice D) is a catecholamine hormone and neurotransmitter. Its primary role in the brain is to promote alertness. Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder involves norepinephrine dysregulation, but antisocial personality disorder likely does not.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department after she became agitated and disruptive on an airplane. She was screaming and using profanity, and the plane was forced to land. On arrival, she is agitated and hypervigilant, and her speech is rapid and pressured. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 140/90 mm Hg. Examination shows warm dry skin. The pupils are 8 mm bilaterally. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Anticholinergic agents (eg, diphenhydramine) commonly cause cognitive impairment, hallucinations, sedation, ataxia, visual disturbances (cycloplegia, or loss of accommodation), dry mouth, tachycardia, urinary retention, constipation, and flushed skin. Offending medications should be discontinued. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Acetylcholine activity in the brain mediates attention and motivation, and peripheral acetylcholine mediates smooth muscle contraction. Diphenhydramine, an antihistamine medication with strong anticholinergic activity, is commonly utilized off-label for acute episodes of anxiety (eg, secondary to flying). Anticholinergic agents commonly cause delirium (as evidenced by this patient's agitated behavior) along with smooth muscle dysfunction (decreased function of sweat glands and pupillary muscles leading to this patient's flushed skin and mydriasis, respectively). Anticholinergic toxicity typically manifests as delirium, sedation, hallucinations, ataxia, mydriasis, visual disturbances (cycloplegia, or loss of accommodation), dry mouth, tachycardia, urinary retention, constipation, and flushed skin. Management includes discontinuation of the offending agent, supportive care with gentle sedation, fluids, and prevention of hyperthermia, seizure, or arrhythmias. In severe cases, treatment with pyridostigmine, a reversible acetylcholinesterase inhibitor, may be initiated.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Acetaminophen overdose (Choice A) most commonly presents with nausea, vomiting, lethargy, and acute liver failure (manifesting with right upper quadrant tenderness, jaundice, encephalopathy, bleeding complications, and increased hepatic transaminases). Tachycardia, mydriasis, and warm dry skin are more consistent with anticholinergic-induced delirium. Benzodiazepine overdose (Choice C) typically presents with central nervous system (CNS) depression, slurred speech, ataxia, emotional lability, and memory lapses. Agitation, tachycardia, and warm, dry skin would be atypical. Lab Values Calculator F) Pulmonary embolism Correct Answer: B. Tachycardia, mydriasis, and warm dry skin are more consistent with anticholinergic-induced delirium. Benzodiazepine overdose (Choice C) typically presents with central nervous system (CNS) depression, slurred speech, ataxia, emotional lability, and memory lapses. Agitation, tachycardia, and warm, dry skin would be atypical. Hypoglycemia (Choice D) may manifest with sympathoadrenal symptoms (tremors, palpitations, diaphoresis, mild hypertension, mild tachycardia) along with cognitive impairment. Warm, dry skin is more consistent with anticholinergic toxicity. Narcotic overdose (Choice E), or overdose with opioids, causes symptoms of CNS depression including sedation, respiratory depression, bradycardia, and hypotension. Miosis differentiates opioid intoxication from other toxidromes, including anticholinergic toxicity. Also, agitation, tachycardia, and warm, dry skin would be atypical.
