questions.stem: 4 7. A 17-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department after attempting suicide by stabbing herself. Her father died suddenly 1 week ago, and she states that she heard his voice telling her to join him. Her mother reports that the patient became wildly agitated yesterday while visiting her father's grave. After arriving home, she sat alone in a corner, rocked back and forth, and talked about "the Devil" taking her soul. Her mother says that she does well in school and has many friends. She has no history of medical or psychiatric illness. Physical examination shows a self-inflicted superficial stab wound over the abdomen. On mental status examination, she talks about seeing angels and devils. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for these findings?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Adjustment disorderwith mixed disturbance of emotions and conduct

questions.educationalObjective: Brief psychotic disorder is characterized by at least one acute psychotic symptom (eg, delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior) lasting less than 1 month. Stressors, such as loss of a loved one, are common triggers. Lab Values Calculator

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient meets the diagnostic criteria for brief psychotic disorder. Brief psychotic disorder is characterized by the acute onset of one or more psychotic symptoms (eg, delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior) lasting less than 1 month. This patient is experiencing a religious delusion, disorganized behavior (eg, agitation, sitting alone in a corner rocking back and forth, stabbing herself), and visual and auditory hallucinations. Risk factors for brief psychotic disorder include stressful life events and personality disorders. Treatment may include hospitalization and antipsychotic treatment depending on symptom severity and functional impairment, though some symptoms or the entire presentation may resolve without medications.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Adjustment disorder (Choice A) refers to the onset of disproportionate distress within 3 months of an identifiable stressor and may be associated with anxiety, depression, or conduct disturbances. Psychotic symptoms such as delusions and hallucinations are atypical. Bereavement (Choice B), or uncomplicated grief reaction, refers to a person's emotional response after a loved one dies and can include sadness, shock, anguish, guilt, difficulty concentrating, decreased interest in usual activities, preoccupation with thoughts and memories of the deceased, and neurovegetative symptoms such as sleep, appetite, and energy disturbances. Hallucinations of the deceased may also occur. Complex religious delusions, disorganization of behavior, and hallucinations other than hallucinations of the deceased are atypical. Lab Values Calculator Correct Answer: C. This patient meets the diagnostic criteria for brief psychotic disorder. Brief psychotic disorder is characterized by the acute onset of one or more psychotic symptoms (eg, delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior) lasting less than 1 month. This patient is experiencing a religious delusion, disorganized behavior (eg, agitation, sitting alone in a corner rocking back and forth, stabbing herself), and visual and auditory hallucinations. Risk factors for brief psychotic disorder include stressful life events and personality disorders. Treatment may include hospitalization and antipsychotic treatment depending on symptom severity and functional impairment, though some symptoms or the entire presentation may resolve without medications. Incorrect Answers: A, B, D, E, and F. Adjustment disorder (Choice A) refers to the onset of disproportionate distress within 3 months of an identifiable stressor and may be associated with anxiety, depression, or conduct disturbances. Psychotic symptoms such as delusions and hallucinations are atypical. Bereavement (Choice B), or uncomplicated grief reaction, refers to a person's emotional response after a loved one dies and can include sadness, shock, anguish, guilt, difficulty concentrating, decreased interest in usual activities, preoccupation with thoughts and memories of the deceased, and neurovegetative symptoms such as sleep, appetite, and energy disturbances. Hallucinations of the deceased may also occur. Complex religious delusions, disorganization of behavior, and hallucinations other than hallucinations of the deceased are atypical. Major depressive disorder with psychotic features (Choice D) includes 2 or more weeks of depressive symptoms (eg, depressive symptoms, neurovegetative symptoms) and psychotic symptoms that occur concurrently. The psychotic symptoms are typically mood congruent (eg, delusions of guilt) and can be conceptualized as secondary to depression. Disorganized behavior and complex religious delusions are more consistent with a psychotic disorder. This patient also does not demonstrate depressed mood or neurovegetative symptoms consistent with major depressive disorder. Schizoaffective disorder (Choice E) is characterized by psychotic symptoms (eg, delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, or negative symptoms) along with significant mood episodes (major depressive, manic episodes). The total duration of symptoms must exceed 6 months. This patient does not demonstrate mood changes or neurovegetative disturbances, and she does not meet the duration criteria of schizoaffective disorder. Separation anxiety disorder (Choice F) refers to excessive fear of separation from an attachment figure. Psychotic symptoms are atypical.

questions.stem:
An 82-year-old man is brought to the physician by his daughter because of progressive problems with his memory during the past 3 years. She became concerned when his electricity was turned off recently after he failed to pay the bill. The patient says that he is not concerned about his memory and that his daughter worries too much. He has no history of serious medical or psychiatric illness. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination, including neurologic examination, shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he has a euthymic mood and reactive affect. He makes numerous paraphasic errors, and his thought process is circumstantial. He recalls one of three words after 5 minutes. He is able to remember his date of birth, the name of his high school, and the year he was married. The patient's drawing of the intersecting triangles in Figure 1 is shown in Figure 2.
[FIGURE: q48_fig1] His Mini-Mental State Examination score has decreased from 26/30 1 year ago to 23/30 today. This patient's cognitive disorder is most likely associated with mutations in genes encoding which of the following?

questions.educationalObjective: Dementia, Alzheimer type, is the most prevalent dementia, beginning with short-term memory impairment and decreased visuospatial abilities, progressing to language abnormalities and executive dysfunction, and culminating in personality changes and the inability to perform basic activities of daily living. The pathogenesis of dementia, Alzheimer type, involves the accumulation of tau and amyloid proteins in cholinergic neurons. Lab Values Calculator

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: In dementia, Alzheimer type, a proteinopathy involving amyloid and tau proteins leads to degeneration of cholinergic neurons that mediate attention and learning. Amyloid plaques and abnormal tau accumulation are pathologic markers of dementia, Alzheimer type. Amyloid plaques are extracellular protein accumulations while tau forms intracytoplasmic protein tangles. Dementia, Alzheimer type is the most prevalent dementia, presenting with progressive cognitive decline that begins with short-term memory impairment (more than biographical memory) and decline in visuospatial abilities (as evidenced by this patient's inability to draw intersecting triangles), and progresses to apraxia, language abnormalities (eg, paraphasic Figure 2 Lab Values Calculator ___ _ __________ Correct Answer: E. In dementia, Alzheimer type, a proteinopathy involving amyloid and tau proteins leads to degeneration of cholinergic neurons that mediate attention and learning. Amyloid plaques and abnormal tau accumulation are pathologic markers of dementia, Alzheimer type. Amyloid plaques are extracellular protein accumulations while tau forms intracytoplasmic protein tangles. Dementia, Alzheimer type is the most prevalent dementia, presenting with progressive cognitive decline that begins with short-term memory impairment (more than biographical memory) and decline in visuospatial abilities (as evidenced by this patient's inability to draw intersecting triangles), and progresses to apraxia, language abnormalities (eg, paraphasic errors), and dysexecutive syndrome (contributing to this patient's inability to pay his bills). The disease culminates in behavioral and personality changes that prevent the patient from performing basic activities of daily living such as bathing. Donepezil and other cholinesterase inhibitors noncompetitively and reversibly inhibit acetylcholinesterase activity and thereby increase the amount of synaptic acetylcholine available for neurotransmission.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Abnormal huntingtin proteins (Choice A), resulting from a mutated huntingtin gene, accumulate in neurons and lead to Huntington disease. Huntington disease is characterized by progressive choreoathetoid movements and dementia. This patient does not demonstrate choreoathetoid movements. Abnormal prion proteins (Choice B), resulting in some patients from hereditary genetic mutations, lead to neurodegenerative encephalopathies (eg, Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease). questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with angina pectoris comes for a follow-up examination. His wife reports that he is concerned that his neighbors have been watching and videotaping him over the past 3 months. He has no history of these concerns prior to this time. He has suggested to his wife that they build a 14-foot fence to stop this surveillance, but she is not concerned that they are being videotaped. He believes that the neighbors might have followed him to his appointment today, but he does not believe that they would come into the office and speak to the office personnel. His medications include metoprolol and aspirin. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he is guarded and hypervigilant. He is oriented to person, place, and time. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Mood disorderdue to a general medical condition

questions.educationalObjective: Delusional disorder is characterized by the presence of one or more delusions (fixed, false beliefs) for a month or longer without other psychotic symptoms. Patients typically lack significant negative symptoms or functional impairment. Lab Values Calculator .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Delusional disorder is characterized by the presence of one or more delusions (fixed, false beliefs) for a month or longer without other psychotic symptoms (eg, hallucinations). Patients typically lack significant negative symptoms (eg, alogia, apathy) or functional impairment. The onset of psychotic symptoms in patients older than 45 years is often consistent with delusional disorder (or a medical cause of psychosis) rather than schizophrenia. The disorder may present suddenly or gradually. Patients typically lack insight into their illness and may resist seeking psychiatric help, though antipsychotic medication and psychotherapy are the preferred treatments.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Brief psychotic disorder (Choice A) is characterized by at least one acute psychotic symptom (eg, delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior) lasting less than 1 month. Mood disorder due to a general medical condition (Choice C) refers to depression or mania secondary to a medical disease. This patient does not demonstrate symptoms of depression or mania, instead presenting with an isolated delusion. Schizotypal personality disorder (Choice D) is a cluster A personality disorder, the odd or eccentric cluster. The disorder is characterized by odd or magical behavior and thinking, along with a constricted affect. Rigid delusions are more consistent with delusional disorder. As well, patients with personality disorders demonstrate a persistent pattern of interpersonal difficulties that typically begins early in life. questions.stem: A 19-year-old college sophomore comes to the physician because of episodes of shortness of breath, palpitations, and sweating that have awakened him from sleep several times over the past 2 months. He says that the episodes are so alarming that he thinks he is going to die and is afraid that he has a serious illness. He reports episodes of sadness since he broke up with his girlfriend 3 weeks ago. He does not smoke, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs. He requests a bronchodilator inhaler. His pulse is 80/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 100/70 mm Hg. The lungs are clear to auscultation. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Panic disorder is a relatively common psychiatric disorder characterized by recurrent panic attacks that are unexpected and associated with worry about future panic attacks or avoidance of panic attack triggers. Panic attacks feature acute fear or anxiety that peaks within minutes and is associated with four additional physical symptoms (eg, shortness of breath, palpitations) or associated mental states (eg, fear of dying). Panic attacks may occur during calm states, including during the nighttime. Lab Values Calculator

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Panic attacks feature acute fear or anxiety that peaks within minutes and is associated with four additional physical symptoms or associated mental states. These additional symptoms may include heart palpitations or tachycardia, shortness of breath, chest pain, dizziness, the sensation of choking, gastrointestinal distress, paresthesias, sweating, chills, trembling, derealization, the fear of dying, or the fear of losing control. These episodes may occur during calm or anxious states, in the daytime or nighttime. The pathogenesis involves genetic factors, an anxious temperament, and stressful life events. In patients with an initial episode of panic symptoms, a thorough history and physical examination along with a basic laboratory workup and a potential ECG should be performed. Panic disorder is a relatively common psychiatric disorder characterized by recurrent panic attacks that are unexpected and associated with worry about future panic attacks or avoidance of panic attack triggers. Treatment of panic disorder typically includes antidepressant medication and/or cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) with a focus on graded exposure to the feared situation.

Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, E, F, and G. Adjustment disorder (Choice A) refers to the onset of disproportionate distress within three months of an identifiable stressor and may be associated with anxiety, depression, and/or conduct disturbances. Separation anxiety disorder (Choice F) refers to excessive fear of separation from an attachment figure. This patient's panic attacks started before his relationship breakup. In factitious disorder (Choice B), patients consciously produce symptoms (eg, purposely injure themselves, fake a fever by eating something warm) for primary gain. A history of multiple procedures and subjective or objective findings that are inconsistent with known disease suggests factitious disorder. This patient's symptoms are consistent with panic disorder. Generalized anxiety disorder (Choice C) is an anxiety disorder that features excessive worrying about a range of topics with associated functional impairment. This patient does not demonstrate a wide range of worries, as his worry about having a serious illness occurs during panic attacks specifically. Post-traumatic stress disorder (Choice E) refers to symptoms of intrusive thoughts and memories of trauma, hyperarousal, avoidance of trauma reminders, or negative mood/thoughts resulting from a traumatic event (such as a life-threatening motor vehicle collision) and lasting more than 1 month. This patient did not experience a life-threatening incident, intrusive memories, or avoidance of trauma reminders. In somatization disorder (Choice G), more commonly known as somatic symptom disorder, the patient is excessively preoccupied with one or more somatic symptoms such that these symptoms disrupt the patient's daily life. Patients persistently devote excessive time and energy to these symptoms or related health concerns (eg, repeatedly going to the doctor). This patient has not demonstrated excessive care-seeking behaviors in the past 2 months.
